11 (c) Implement a regional process to rank career clusters in
12 the workforce development board service delivery area as described
13 under subsection (4). Regional processes must be approved by the
14 department before the ranking of career clusters.
15 (d) Report CTE early middle college and CTE dual enrollment
16 program and student data and information as prescribed by the
17 department and the center.
18 (e) The local education agency responsible for student
19 reporting in the Michigan student data system (MSDS) will report
20 the total number of college credits the student earned, at the time
21 of high school graduation, as determined by the department and the
22 center.
23 (f) The local education agency will report each award outcome
24 in the Michigan student data system (MSDS) that the CTE early
25 middle college student attained. For purposes of this subsection,
26 an on-track CTE early middle college graduate is a graduate who
27 obtained their high school diploma and at least 1 of the following:
28 (i) An associate degree.
29 (ii) 60 transferable college credits.
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) e6cdo0
277
1 (iii) Professional certification.
2 (iv) A Michigan Early Middle College Association certificate.
3 (v) Participation in a registered apprenticeship.
4 (4) A regional strategic plan must be approved by the career
5 and educational advisory council before submission to the
6 department. A regional strategic plan must include, but is not
7 limited to, the following:
8 (a) An identification of regional employer need based on a
9 ranking of all career clusters in the workforce development board
10 service delivery area ranked by 10-year projections of annual job
11 openings and median wage for each standard occupational code in
12 each career cluster as obtained from the United States Bureau of
13 Labor Statistics. Standard occupational codes within high-ranking
14 clusters also may be further ranked by median wage and annual job
15 openings. The career and educational advisory council located in
16 the workforce development board service delivery area shall review
17 the rankings and modify them if necessary to accurately reflect
18 employer demand for talent in the workforce development board
19 service delivery area. A career and educational advisory council
20 shall document that it has conducted this review and certify that
21 it is accurate. These career cluster rankings must be determined
22 and updated once every 4 years.
23 (b) An identification of educational entities in the workforce
24 development board service delivery area that will provide eligible
25 CTE early middle college and CTE dual enrollment programs including
26 districts, intermediate districts, postsecondary institutions, and
27 noncredit occupational training programs leading to an industry-
28 recognized credential.
29 (c) A strategy to inform parents and students of CTE early
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) e6cdo0
278
1 middle college and CTE dual enrollment programs in the workforce
2 development board service delivery area.
3 (d) Any other requirements as defined by the department.
4 (5) An eligible CTE program is a program that meets all of the
5 following:
6 (a) Has been identified in the highest 5 career cluster
7 rankings in any of the 16 workforce development board service
8 delivery area strategic plans jointly approved by the department of
9 labor and economic opportunity and the department.
10 (b) Has a coherent sequence of courses in a specific career
11 cluster that will allow a student to earn a high school diploma and
12 achieve at least 1 of the following:
13 (i) For CTE early middle college, outcomes as defined in
14 subsection (3)(f).
15 (ii) For CTE dual enrollment, 1 of the following:
16 (A) An associate degree.
17 (B) An industry-recognized technical certification approved by
18 the department of labor and economic opportunity.
19 (C) Up to 60 transferable college credits.
20 (D) Participation in a registered apprenticeship, pre-
21 apprenticeship, or apprentice readiness program.
22 (c) Is aligned with the Michigan merit curriculum.
23 (d) Has an articulation or a college credit agreement with at
24 least 1 postsecondary institution that provides students with
25 opportunities to receive postsecondary credits during the student's
26 participation in the CTE early middle college or CTE dual
27 enrollment program and transfers those credits to the postsecondary
28 institution upon completion of the CTE early middle college or CTE
29 dual enrollment program.
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) e6cdo0
279
1 (e) Provides instruction that is supervised, directed, or
2 coordinated by an appropriately certificated CTE teacher or, for
3 concurrent enrollment courses, a postsecondary faculty member.
4 (f) Provides for highly integrated student support services
5 that include at least the following:
6 (i) Teachers as academic advisors.
7 (ii) Supervised course selection.
8 (iii) Monitoring of student progress and completion.
9 (iv) Career planning services provided by a local one-stop
10 service center as described in the Michigan works one-stop service
11 center system act, 2006 PA 491, MCL 408.111 to 408.135, or by a
12 high school counselor or advisor.
13 (g) Has courses that are taught on a college campus, are
14 college courses offered at the high school and taught by college
15 faculty, or are courses taught in combination with online
16 instruction.
17 (6) The department shall distribute funds to eligible CTE
18 early middle college and CTE dual enrollment programs as follows:
19 (a) The department shall determine statewide average CTE costs
20 per pupil for each CIP code program by calculating statewide
21 average costs for each CIP code program for the 3 most recent
22 fiscal years.
23 (b) The distribution to each eligible CTE early middle college
24 or CTE dual enrollment program is the product of 50% of CTE costs
25 per pupil times the pupil enrollment of each eligible CTE early
26 middle college or CTE dual enrollment program in the immediately
27 preceding school year.

2 Sec. 61w. (1) From the state school aid fund money
3 appropriated in section 11, there is allocated for 2026-2027 only
4 an amount not to exceed $5,000,000.00 for competitive grants to
5 districts and intermediate districts to support virtual reality
6 simulation programs that enhance and expand existing career and
7 technical education programs.
8 (2) Eligible districts and intermediate districts shall apply
9 for funds under this section on a competitive basis in a form and
10 manner determined by the department.
11 (3) A virtual reality simulation program described in this
12 section must offer tactile, multimodal learning experiences that
13 derive and teach core state standards-aligned concepts and skills
14 for grades 6 to 12 math and science, including middle school math
15 and algebra I. The virtual reality simulation program should
16 immerse students in real-world problems across the economy to build
17 career exposure and relevance, using 3-D models and interactives
18 that visualize and manipulate abstract mathematical concepts that
19 students typically struggle to grasp, retain, and apply.
20 (4) Awards received under this section must be used to support
21 career and technical education programs aligned with high-skill,
22 high-wage, and high-demand occupations across career clusters
23 aligned with local and market needs. A recipient may use an award
24 under this section to support virtual reality simulation programs
25 that reach multiple career clusters.
26 (5) Notwithstanding section 17b, the department shall make
27 payments under this section on a schedule determined by the
28 department.

11 Sec. 67. (1) From the general fund money appropriated in

16 intended to increase the number of pupils who are adequately
17 prepared with the information needed to make informed decisions on
18 college and career, support adult learners, support college
19 completion, and support workforce and employer engagement. The
20 funds appropriated under this section are intended to be used to
21 increase the number of Michigan residents with high-quality degrees
22 or credentials. Funds appropriated under this section must not be
23 used to supplant funding for counselors already funded by
24 districts.

26 potential shall administer funds allocated under this section in
27 collaboration with the Michigan college access network. These funds

29 (a) Michigan college access network operations, programming,
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) e6cdo0
289
1 and services to local college access networks.
2 (b) Local college access networks, which are community-based
3 college access/success partnerships committed to increasing the
4 college participation and completion rates within geographically
5 defined communities through a coordinated strategy.
6 (c) The Michigan college advising program, a program intended
7 to place trained, recently graduated college advisors in high
8 schools that serve significant numbers of low-income and first-
9 generation college-going pupils. State funds used for this purpose
10 may not exceed 33% of the total funds available under this
11 subsection.
12 (d) Subgrants of up to $5,000.00 to districts with
13 comprehensive high schools that establish a college access team and
14 implement specific strategies to create a college-going culture in
15 a high school in a form and manner approved by the Michigan college
16 access network and the department of lifelong education,
17 advancement, and potential.
18 (e) The Michigan college access portal, an online one-stop
19 portal to help pupils and families plan and apply for college.
20 (f) Public awareness and outreach campaigns to encourage low-
21 income and first-generation college-going pupils to take necessary
22 steps toward college and to assist pupils and families in
23 completing a timely and accurate free application for federal
24 student aid.
25 (g) Subgrants to postsecondary institutions to recruit, hire,
26 and train college student mentors and college advisors to assist

1 Sec. 94a. (1) There is created within the state budget office
2 in the department of technology, management, and budget the center
3 for educational performance and information. The center shall do
4 all of the following:
5 (a) Coordinate the collection of all data required by state
6 and federal law from districts, intermediate districts, and
7 postsecondary institutions.
8 (b) Create, maintain, and enhance this state's P-20
9 longitudinal data system and ensure that it meets the requirements
10 of subsection (4).
11 (c) Collect data in the most efficient manner possible to
12 reduce the administrative burden on reporting entities, including,
13 but not limited to, electronic transcript services.
14 (d) Create, maintain, and enhance this state's web-based
15 educational portal to provide information to school leaders,
16 teachers, researchers, and the public in compliance with all
17 federal and state privacy laws. Data must include, but are not
18 limited to, all of the following:
19 (i) Data sets that link teachers to student information,
20 allowing districts to assess individual teacher impact on student
21 performance and consider student growth factors in teacher and
22 principal evaluation systems.
23 (ii) Data access or, if practical, data sets, provided for
24 regional data hubs that, in combination with local data, can
25 improve teaching and learning in the classroom.
26 (iii) Research-ready data sets for researchers to perform
27 research that advances this state's educational performance.
28 (e) Provide data in a useful manner to allow state and local
29 policymakers to make informed policy decisions.

297
1 (f) Provide public reports to the residents of this state to

19 (4) The center shall ensure that the P-20 longitudinal data
20 system required under subsection (1)(b) meets all of the following:
21 (a) Includes data at the individual student level from
22 preschool through postsecondary education and into the workforce.
23 (b) Supports interoperability by using standard data
24 structures, data formats, and data definitions to ensure linkage
25 and connectivity in a manner that facilitates the exchange of data
26 among agencies and institutions within the state and between
27 states.
28 (c) Enables the matching of individual teacher and student
29 records so that an individual student may be matched with those
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) e6cdo0
298
1 teachers providing instruction to that student.
2 (d) Enables the matching of individual teachers with
3 information about their certification and the institutions that
4 prepared and recommended those teachers for state certification.
5 (e) Enables data to be easily generated for continuous
6 improvement and decision-making, including timely reporting to
7 parents, teachers, and school leaders on student achievement.
8 (f) Ensures the reasonable quality, validity, and reliability
9 of data contained in the system.
10 (g) Provides this state with the ability to meet federal and

13 and postsecondary, meets all of the following:
14 (i) Contains a unique statewide student identifier that does

CRM H06167'26 (H-1) e6cdo0

6 systems.
7 (i) For data elements related to preschool through grade 12

16 (12) As used in this section:
17 (a) "Blended learning" means a hybrid instructional delivery
18 model where pupils are provided content, instruction, and
19 assessment, in part at a supervised educational facility away from
20 home where the pupil and a teacher with a valid Michigan teaching
21 certificate are in the same physical location and in part through
22 internet-connected learning environments with some degree of pupil
23 control over time, location, and pace of instruction.
24 (b) "Cyber school" means a full-time instructional program of
25 virtual courses for pupils that may or may not require attendance
26 at a physical school location.
27 (c) "Virtual course" means a course of study that is capable
28 of generating a credit or a grade and that is provided in an
29 interactive learning environment in which the majority of the
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) e6cdo0
313
1 curriculum is delivered using the internet and in which pupils are
2 separated from their instructor or teacher of record by time or
3 location, or both.

1 section 17b, the department shall make grant payments to districts,

6 that will be able to receive funds and expands the geographical
7 distribution of teams. Districts and intermediate districts that
8 receive funds under this section must provide relevant student
9 participation information, as determined by the department, to
10 program and competition providers described in this section. For a
11 district or intermediate district to count a program competition
12 provider for purposes of payments under this section, the program
13 and competition providers must agree to aggregate data received by
14 districts and intermediate districts and provide this information
15 to the department in a form and manner determined by the
16 department.
17 (2) The department shall do all of the following for purposes
18 of this section:
19 (a) Both of the following by not later than 60 days after the
20 state school aid appropriations bill for the current fiscal year is
21 enacted into law or October 1 of the current fiscal year, whichever
22 is later:
23 (i) Open applications for funding under this section to all
24 districts, nonpublic schools, and intermediate districts.
25 (ii) Publish a list of approved programs and vendors for
26 purposes of this section in a manner that is accessible to all
27 applicants. To obtain approval under this subparagraph, a program
28 or vendor must submit to the department registration information,
29 including any fees; pledge that it will post this information on
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) e6cdo0
315
1 its website; and, by not later than January 1 of the current fiscal
2 year, submit this information to the department for publication on
3 the department's website.
4 (b) By not later than 60 days after applications are opened as
5 described in subdivision (a), close applications under this
6 section.
7 (c) By not later than 60 days after applications are closed as
8 described in subdivision (b), make all determinations concerning
9 funding under this section.
10 (d) By not later than July 1 of the current fiscal year,
11 publish a document listing the requirements for becoming an
12 approved program or vendor under subdivision (a).
13 (3) Except as otherwise provided under this subsection, if
14 funding under this section is insufficient to fulfill all funding
15 requests by qualified applicants under this section, the department
16 shall prorate the total funding allocated under this section
17 equally among all qualified applicants. However, for funding under
18 this section toward grants under subsection (5)(b), in its
19 proration under this subsection, the department shall ensure that
20 each district is paid in an amount equal to the percentage the
21 department would have paid the district in grant funding under
22 subsection (5)(b), but for proration under this subsection, with no
23 district receiving a grant under subsection (5)(b) in an amount
24 that is greater than the district's total accrued costs under
25 subsection (5)(b).
26 (4) A district, nonpublic school, or intermediate district
27 applying for a grant under this section must submit an application
28 in a form and manner prescribed by the department. To be eligible
29 for a grant, a district, nonpublic school, or intermediate district
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) e6cdo0
316
1 must demonstrate in its application that the district, nonpublic
2 school, or intermediate district has established a partnership for
3 the purposes of the robotics program with at least 1 sponsor,
4 business entity, higher education institution, technical school, or
5 individual, must submit a budget, and must provide a local in-kind
6 or cash match from other private or local funds of at least 25% of
7 the cost of the robotics program award.
8 (5) The department shall distribute the grant funding under
9 this section for the following purposes:
10 (a) Grants to districts, nonpublic schools, or intermediate
11 districts to pay for stipends not to exceed $1,500.00 per building
12 for coaching.
13 (b) Grants to districts, nonpublic schools, or intermediate
14 districts for event registrations, materials, travel costs, and
15 other expenses associated with the preparation for and attendance
16 at robotics events and competitions.
17 (c) Grants to districts, nonpublic schools, or intermediate
18 districts for awards to teams that advance to the next levels of
19 competition as determined by the department. The department shall
20 determine an equal amount per team for those teams that advance.
21 (6) A nonpublic school that receives a grant under this
22 section may use the funds for either robotics or Science Olympiad
23 programs.
24 (7) To be eligible to receive funds under this section, a
25 nonpublic school must be a nonpublic school registered with the
26 department and must meet all applicable state reporting
27 requirements for nonpublic schools.

1 hours and days of pupil instruction under this subsection, the

22 (4) All of the following apply to the counting of days and
23 hours of pupil instruction under this section:
24 (a) Except as otherwise provided in this subsection, the first
25 6 days or the equivalent number of hours for which pupil
26 instruction is not provided because of conditions not within the
27 control of school authorities, such as severe storms, fires,
28 epidemics, utility power unavailability, water or sewer failure, or
29 health conditions as defined by the city, county, or state health
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) e6cdo0
324
1 authorities, are counted as hours and days of pupil instruction.

3 instruction, the department shall count as hours and days of pupil
4 instruction for a fiscal year not more than 3 additional days or
5 the equivalent number of additional hours for which pupil
6 instruction is not provided in a district due to unusual and
7 extenuating occurrences resulting from conditions not within the
8 control of school authorities such as those conditions described in
9 this subsection.
10 (c) A district that counts hours or days of professional
11 development for teachers as hours or days of pupil instruction, as
12 provided under subsection (10), is eligible to have additional
13 hours or days counted as hours and days of pupil instruction as
14 provided under subdivision (b) to the same extent as a district
15 that does not count hours or days of professional development for
16 teachers as hours or days of pupil instruction.
17 (d) In deciding whether or not to approve the counting of
18 additional hours or days of pupil instruction under subdivision (b)
19 for a district, the superintendent of public instruction shall not
20 take into account whether or not the district counts hours or days
21 of professional development for teachers as hours or days of pupil
22 instruction, as provided under subsection (10).
23 (e) Subsequent hours or days beyond those described in
24 subdivisions (a) and (b) are not counted as hours or days of pupil
25 instruction.

20 instruction required under subsection (3), a district shall use the
21 following guidelines, and a district shall maintain records to
22 substantiate its compliance with the following guidelines:

24 must be scheduled for at least the required minimum number of hours
25 of instruction, excluding study halls, or at least the sum of 90
26 hours plus the required minimum number of hours of instruction,

29 block schedule may be considered instructional time, unless that
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) e6cdo0

1 time is determined in an audit to be a study hall period.

6 the individual pupil's best educational interest due to the pupil's
7 participation in an advanced curriculum, must be scheduled for a

9 number of hours of pupil instruction to be considered a full-time

11 4-block schedule may receive a reduced schedule under this
12 subsection if the pupil is scheduled for a number of hours equal to
13 at least 75% of the required minimum number of hours of pupil
14 instruction to be considered a full-time equivalent pupil.

16 (10) A district may count up to 38 hours of professional
17 development for teachers as hours of pupil instruction. All of the
18 following apply to the counting of professional development as
19 pupil instruction under this subsection:
20 (a) If the professional development exceeds 5 hours in a
21 single day, that day may be counted as a day of pupil instruction.
22 (b) At least 8 hours of the professional development counted
23 as hours of pupil instruction under this subsection must be
24 recommended by a districtwide professional development advisory
25 committee appointed by the district board. The advisory committee
26 must be composed of teachers employed by the district who represent
27 a variety of grades and subject matter specializations, including
28 special education; nonteaching staff; parents; and administrators.
29 The majority membership of the committee must be composed of
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) e6cdo0
329
1 teaching staff.
2 (c) Professional development provided online is allowable and
3 encouraged, as long as the instruction has been approved by the
4 district. The department shall issue a list of approved online
5 professional development providers that must include the Michigan
6 Virtual School.
7 (d) Professional development may only be counted as hours of
8 pupil instruction under this subsection for the pupils of those
9 teachers scheduled to participate in the professional development.
10 (e) The professional development must meet all of the
11 following to be counted as pupil instruction under this subsection:
12 (i) Be aligned to the school or district improvement plan for
13 the school or district in which the professional development is
14 being provided.
15 (ii) Be linked to 1 or more criteria in the evaluation tool
16 developed or adopted by the district or intermediate district under
17 section 1249 of the revised school code, MCL 380.1249.
18 (iii) Has been approved by the department as counting for state
19 continuing education clock hours. The number of hours of
20 professional development counted as hours of pupil instruction
21 under this subsection may not exceed the number of state continuing
22 education clock hours for which the professional development was
23 approved.
24 (iv) Not more than a combined total of 10 hours of the
25 professional development takes place before the first scheduled day
26 of school for the school year ending in the fiscal year and after
27 the last scheduled day of school for that school year.
28 (v) Not more than 10 hours of the professional development
29 takes place in a single month.

20 Sec. 104h. (1) From the state school aid fund money

25 timely manner.

21 charge any applicable labor charge amount to cover or partially
22 cover the cost of health or fringe benefits. A nonpublic school

24 costs.

23 documentation has been submitted and reviewed.
24 (b) Verify to the extent possible that a grant recipient is a
25 not-for-profit entity and will use funds as publicly disclosed and
26 for a public purpose that serves the economic prosperity, health,

368
1 perform its fiduciary duty, and comply with all applicable state

6 schedule in the executed grant agreement on a reimbursement basis
7 after the grantee has provided sufficient documentation, as
8 determined by the responsible entity, to verify that expenditures
9 were made in accordance with the project purpose.
10 (e) If the state budget director determines that information
11 provided by the grantee does not meet the disclosure requirements,
12 that the grant will be used to pay a tax lien, delinquent tax, or
13 other obligation owed to this state, or that the grant will create
14 a conflict of interest, the responsible entity shall not release
15 the grant money to the grantee. Money that is not released under

4 as determined by the responsible entity. The grant agreement
5 required under this section must include signed assurance by the
6 chief executive officer or other executive officer of the grant
7 recipient authorized to bind the grant recipient that the
8 requirements of this subsection will be met.
9 (6) The grant recipient shall expend all grant money awarded
10 and complete all projects not later than September 30, 2030. If, at
11 that time, any unexpended money remains, the grant recipient shall
12 return that money to the state treasury. If a grant recipient does
13 not provide information sufficient to execute a grant agreement not
14 later than June 1, 2026, the responsible entity shall return money
15 associated with the grant to the state treasury.
16 (7) Any grant money that is awarded to a responsible entity is
17 appropriated in that responsible entity for the purpose of the
18 intended grant.
19 (8) Except as otherwise provided in subsection (9), beginning
20 March 15 of the current fiscal year, the responsible entity shall
21 post a report in a publicly accessible location on its website. The
22 report must list the grant recipient, project purpose, and location
23 of the project for each grant described in subsection (1), the
24 status of money allocated and disbursed under the grant agreement,
25 and the legislative sponsor, if applicable. The responsible entity
26 shall update the report and post the updated report in a publicly
27 accessible location on its website not later than June 15 of the
28 current fiscal year and again not later than September 15 of the
29 current fiscal year. The responsible entity shall include in the
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) e6cdo0
371
1 report the most comprehensive information the responsible entity
2 has available at the time of posting for grants awarded.
3 (9) If the state budget office determines that it is more
4 efficient for the state budget office to compile all affected
5 responsible entities' information and post a report of the compiled
6 information rather than the report required under subsection (8)
7 being posted by individual responsible entities, the state budget
8 office may compile that information across all affected responsible
9 entities and other state departments and post the compiled report
10 and any updates on the same time schedule as identified in
11 subsection (8).
12 (10) If the responsible entity reasonably determines that the
13 money allocated for an executed grant agreement under this section
14 was misused or that use of the money was misrepresented by the
15 grant recipient, the responsible entity shall not award any
16 additional funds under the executed grant agreement and shall refer
17 the grant for review following internal audit protocols, which may
18 include referral for criminal investigation.
19 (11) As used in this section, "responsible entity" means the
20 department, the department of lifelong education, advancement, and
21 potential, a district, an intermediate district, or other person
22 that administers a grant under this article.state budget office
23 shall post a list of legislatively directed spending items funded
24 under this article as defined in sections 364 and 364a of the
25 management and budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1364 and 18.1364a.

27 the funded items as publicly disclosed in accordance with sections
28 364 and 364a of the management and budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL

1 (2) In accordance with section 364(4) of the management and

11 (3) Before executing a grant agreement, all grant recipients
12 must provide a spending plan that specifies the scope of service,
13 the program goals, the measurement for meeting program goals, and
14 how all grant funds will be used, and must indicate if any grant
15 funds will be provided to a third party or subrecipient.
16 (4) Each department or agency responsible for awarding or
17 disbursing grant funds must annually audit the entity's use of the
18 grant funds for each fiscal year in which the grant is active. Each
19 entity must submit information requested by the department or
20 agency to confirm compliance.
21 (5) Each department or agency shall work with the office of
22 the auditor general to conduct an audit of the grant according to
23 generally accepted accounting practices and audit standards. The
24 office of the auditor general must be given access to all internal
25 and external records. All contracts or agreements between a
26 department or agency and a grant recipient or between a grant
27 recipient and a third party must include a clause granting the
28 office of the auditor general access to all internal and external
29 records.

1 (6) Grant recipients and their immediate family members are
2 prohibited from being employed by the executive branch or
3 legislative branch of this state. In addition, grant recipients are
4 prohibited from serving on any state board that has direct or
5 indirect responsibility for approving or auditing grant funds
6 awarded or disbursed by any department or agency.
7 (7) Full and complete audits of grant funds issued by the
8 department or an agency of this state, without redaction unless

10 website in a conspicuous place for public review.

25 Sec. 164m. Districts and intermediate districts are encouraged
26 to evaluate and pursue efficiency and cost-containment measures
27 that maximize state funding. Districts and intermediate districts
28 shall identify practices that increase efficiencies, including, but
29 not limited to, establishing joint ventures, consolidating

1 services, utilizing program collaborations, maximizing educational
2 benefits through optimal class sizes and frequency of course
3 offerings, eliminating low-enrollment and high-cost instructional
4 programs, using self-insurance, practicing energy conservation, and
5 utilizing group purchasing.

15 Sec. 201i. (1) Not later than 30 days after the enactment of
16 the amendatory act that added this section, the legislature shall
17 provide to the responsible entity and the state budget director a
18 list of legislatively directed spending items, which may be
19 referred to in this section as grants or direct appropriation
20 grants, funded under the amendatory act that added this section
21 consistent with house or senate rules and this section. The list
22 must include all information and documents pertaining to the funded
23 items as publicly disclosed in accordance with house or senate
24 rules and this section.
25 (2) Notwithstanding any other conditions or requirements for
26 direct appropriation grants, the responsible entity shall perform,
27 at a minimum, at least all of the following activities to
28 administer the grants described in subsection (1):
29 (a) Establish a process to review, complete, and execute a
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) e6cdo0
382
1 grant agreement with a grant recipient. The responsible entity
2 shall not execute a grant agreement unless all necessary
3 documentation has been submitted and reviewed.
4 (b) Verify to the extent possible that a grant recipient is a
5 not-for-profit entity and will use funds as publicly disclosed and
6 for a public purpose that serves the economic prosperity, health,
7 safety, or general welfare of the residents of this state.
8 (c) Review and verify all necessary information to ensure the
9 grant recipient is reasonably able to execute the grant agreement,
10 perform its fiduciary duty, and comply with all applicable state
11 and federal statutes. The responsible entity may deduct the cost of
12 background checks and any other efforts performed as part of this
13 verification from the amount of the designated grant award.
14 (d) Disburse the grant money per the grant disbursement
15 schedule in the executed grant agreement on a reimbursement basis
16 after the grantee has provided sufficient documentation, as
17 determined by the responsible entity, to verify that expenditures
18 were made in accordance with the project purpose.
19 (e) If the state budget director determines that information
20 provided by the grantee does not meet the disclosure requirements,
21 that the grant will be used to pay a tax lien, delinquent tax, or
22 other obligation owed to this state, or that the grant will create
23 a conflict of interest, the responsible entity shall not release
24 the grant money to the grantee. Money that is not released under
25 this subdivision lapses at the end of the fiscal year. There is not
26 a conflict of interest if the sponsoring legislator certifies that
27 the sponsoring legislator's immediate family members, legislative
28 staff members that have worked for the sponsoring legislator within
29 the past 2 years, and the sponsoring legislator do not have a
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) e6cdo0
383
1 direct or indirect pecuniary interest in the legislatively directed
2 spending item.
3 (3) An executed grant agreement under this section between the
4 responsible entity and a grant recipient must include at least all
5 of the following:
6 (a) All necessary identifying information for the grant
7 recipient, including any tax and financial information necessary
8 for the responsible entity to administer grant money under this
9 section.
10 (b) A description of the project for which the grant money
11 will be expended, including tentative timelines and the estimated
12 budget. Project budget must include how all grant money will be
13 used and must indicate if any grant money will be provided to a
14 third party or subrecipient. The responsible entity shall not
15 reimburse expenditures that are outside of the project purpose, as
16 stated in the executed grant agreement, from appropriations under
17 the amendatory act that added this section. The grantee shall
18 return to the state treasury any interest in excess of $1,000.00
19 earned on the grant money while unexpended and in possession of the
20 grantee.
21 (c) Unless otherwise specified in the responsible entity's
22 policy, a requirement that funds appropriated for the grants
23 described in subsection (1) may be used only for expenditures that
24 occur on or after the effective date of the amendatory act that
25 added this section.
26 (d) A requirement for reporting by the grant recipient to the

29 the grant recipient, as determined by the responsible entity.

14 single archivable location on an internet site, not later than the

17 fiscal year on the community college's website, a community college
18 shall maintain on its website all reports placed on the website
19 from previous fiscal years, posted by fiscal year in the same
20 single archivable location.

13 Sec. 216c. Funds appropriated in section 201 for
14 infrastructure, technology, equipment, maintenance, and safety are
15 intended to be used for necessary improvements and deferred
16 maintenance of community college buildings, facilities, and other
17 physical infrastructure; necessary improvements and deferred
18 maintenance of information technology, other technology
19 infrastructure, and other equipment; and other purposes related to
20 infrastructure, technology, equipment, and maintenance. A community
21 college may also use these funds to upgrade safety and security
22 infrastructure. These funds are not intended to be used for any
23 other purpose than what is specified in this section.

21 Sec. 217b. (1) Each community college that receives an
22 appropriation in section 201 shall report to the center by the last
23 business day of August of each year the tuition and mandatory fees
24 paid by a full-time in-district student and a full-time out-of-
25 district student as established by the community college governing
26 board for the current academic year. This report should also
27 include the annual cost of tuition and fees based on a full-time
28 course load of 30 credits. This report must also specify the amount
29 that tuition and fees have increased for the community college from
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) e6cdo0
399
1 the prior academic year. Each community college shall also report
2 any revisions to the reported current academic year tuition and
3 mandatory fees adopted by the community college governing board to
4 the center within 15 days of being adopted. The center shall
5 provide this information and any revisions to the house and senate
6 fiscal agencies and the state budget director.standard report
7 recipients.
8 (2) Each community college that receives an appropriation in
9 section 201 shall certify to the state budget director by the first
10 last business day of November August of each year that its board
11 will not adopt an increase in tuition and fee rates for in-district
12 students for the academic year that is greater than the tuition
13 restraint described in this subsection. For the academic year 2025-
14 2026, the tuition restraint level is equal to the greater of 4.5%
15 or $227.00. For the academic year 2026-2027, the tuition restraint
16 level is equal to the greater of 4.0% or $199.00. For the academic
17 year 2027-2028, the tuition restraint level is equal to the greater
18 of 3.0% or $153.00. It is intended that in the next fiscal year,
19 the tuition restraint rate will be adjusted only for the subsequent
20 academic year. As used in this subsection:
21 (a) "Fee" means any board-authorized fee that will be paid by
22 more than 1/2 of all in-district students at least once during
23 their enrollment at a community college. A community college
24 increasing a fee that applies to a specific subset of students or
25 courses shall provide sufficient information to prove that the
26 increase applied to that subset will not cause the increase in the
27 average amount of board-authorized total tuition and fees paid by
28 in-district students in the academic year to exceed the limit
29 established in this section.
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) e6cdo0
400
1 (b) "Tuition and fee rate" means the average of full-time
2 rates paid by a majority of students in each class, based on an
3 unweighted average of the rates authorized by the community college
4 board and actually charged to students, deducting any uniformly
5 rebated or refunded amounts, for the 2 semesters with the highest
6 levels of full-time equated in-district enrollment during the
7 academic year.
8 (3) Community colleges that exceed the tuition and fee rate
9 cap described in subsection (2) are not eligible to receive
10 payments under section 201f 201 for 1-time performance funding
11 infrastructure, technology, equipment, maintenance, and safety
12 payments for fiscal year 2025-2026. 2026-2027. The state budget
13 director shall implement uniform reporting requirements to ensure
14 that a community college receiving a payment under section 201f 201
15 for 1-time performance funding infrastructure, technology,
16 equipment, maintenance, and safety has satisfied the tuition
17 restraint requirements of this section. The state budget director
18 has the sole authority to determine if a community college has met
19 the requirements of this section. Information reported by a
20 community college to the state budget director under this
21 subsection must also be reported to the house and senate
22 appropriations subcommittees on community colleges and the house
23 and senate fiscal agencies.standard report recipients.
24 (4) Notwithstanding any other provision of this act, the
25 legislature may at any time adjust appropriations for a community
26 college that adopts an increase in tuition and fee rates for in-
27 district students that exceeds the rate cap established in
28 subsection (2).
29 Sec. 217f. It is the intent of the legislature that taxpayer
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) e6cdo0
401
1 Taxpayer funds appropriated under this article are to be used to
2 educate community college students and continue maintenance of
3 community college buildings and other assets. The goal of the
4 legislature is that taxpayer funds not be used excessively for
5 administration. Therefore, a community college that receives an
6 appropriation under this article shall not spend more than 10% of
7 that appropriation on administration. A sum of money spent in
8 excess of the 10% cap described in this section by a community
9 college results in a forfeiture of an amount equal to 50% of that
10 sum from funds appropriated to that community college, not to
11 exceed the total amount appropriated to that community college
12 under this article. The auditor general may investigate allegations
13 of violations of this section. Funds forfeited under this section
14 must revert, to the extent permitted by law, to the general fund or
15 the state school aid fund. For the purpose of calculations under
16 this section, the amount spent by a community college on
17 administrative costs does not include costs associated with staff
18 working in the following roles: teachers, facilities, public
19 safety, technology, research, instruction, academic support,
20 student services, auxiliary services, or public service.

7 (2) Money included in the appropriations for community college
8 operations under section 201(2) for local strategic value is
9 allocated only to each community college that certifies to the
10 state budget director, through a board of trustees resolution on or
11 before October 15, 2025, 2026, that the college has met 4 out of 5
12 best practices listed in each category described in subsection (3).
13 The resolution must provide specifics as to how the community
14 college meets each best practice measure within each category. One-
15 third of funding available under the strategic value component is
16 allocated to each category described in subsection (3). Amounts
17 distributed under local strategic value must be on a proportionate
18 basis to each college's fiscal year 2024-2025 2025-2026 operations
19 funding. Payments to community colleges that qualify for local
20 strategic value funding must be distributed with the November
21 installment payment described in section 206.
22 (3) For purposes of subsection (2), the following categories
23 of best practices reflect functional activities of community
24 colleges that have strategic value to the local communities and
25 regional economies:
26 (a) For Category A, economic development and business or
27 industry partnerships, the following:
28 (i) The community college has active partnerships with local
29 employers including hospitals and health care providers.
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) e6cdo0
407
1 (ii) The community college provides customized on-site training
2 for area companies, employees, or both.
3 (iii) The community college supports entrepreneurship through a
4 small business assistance center or other training or consulting
5 activities targeted toward small businesses.
6 (iv) The community college supports technological advancement
7 through industry partnerships, incubation activities, or operation
8 of a Michigan technical education center or other advanced
9 technology center.
10 (v) The community college has active partnerships with local
11 or regional workforce and economic development agencies.
12 (b) For Category B, educational partnerships, the following:
13 (i) The community college has active partnerships with regional
14 high schools, intermediate school districts, and career-tech
15 centers to provide instruction through dual enrollment, concurrent
16 enrollment, direct credit, middle college, or academy programs.
17 (ii) The community college hosts, sponsors, or participates in
18 enrichment programs for area K-12 students, such as college days,
19 summer or after-school programming, or Science Olympiad.
20 (iii) The community college provides, supports, or participates
21 in programming to promote successful transitions to college for
22 traditional age students, including grant programs such as talent
23 search, upward bound, or other activities to promote college
24 readiness in area high schools and community centers.
25 (iv) The community college provides, supports, or participates
26 in programming to promote successful transitions to college for new
27 or reentering adult students, such as adult basic education, a high
28 school equivalency test preparation program and testing, or
29 recruiting, advising, or orientation activities specific to adults.

408
1 As used in this subparagraph, "high school equivalency test

3 (v) The community college has active partnerships with
4 regional 4-year colleges and universities to promote successful

7 (c) For Category C, community services, the following:
8 (i) The community college provides continuing education
9 programming for leisure, wellness, personal enrichment, or
10 professional development.
11 (ii) The community college operates or sponsors opportunities
12 for community members to engage in activities that promote leisure,

16 promote cultural, educational, or personal enrichment for community

20 promote leisure or wellness activities for community members,

25 members.

5 Sec. 236j. (1) The postsecondary scholarship fund is created
6 in the department of treasury for the purpose of providing
7 scholarship awards to eligible students who attend eligible
8 postsecondary educational institutions in this state, as provided
9 in subsection (5).
10 (2) The state treasurer may receive money or other assets from
11 any source for deposit into the postsecondary scholarship fund. The
12 state treasurer shall direct the investment of the postsecondary
13 scholarship fund. The state treasurer shall credit to the
14 postsecondary scholarship fund interest and earnings from
15 postsecondary scholarship fund investments.
16 (3) Money in the postsecondary scholarship fund at the close
17 of the fiscal year must remain in the postsecondary scholarship
18 fund and not lapse to the general fund.
19 (4) The department of treasury is the administrator of the
20 postsecondary scholarship fund for auditing purposes.
21 (5) Money must be expended from the postsecondary scholarship
22 fund only for the purpose of providing Michigan achievement
23 scholarship awards to eligible students who attend eligible
24 postsecondary educational institutions in this state and for other
25 purposes described in this section. From the funds appropriated in
26 section 236(7) for the Michigan achievement scholarship, the
27 department of lifelong education, advancement, and potential may
28 use up to $10,000,000.00 annually for the purposes of outreach
29 programs to raise awareness of the Michigan achievement scholarship
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) e6cdo0
418
1 and other state scholarship programs allocated in section 236(7).
2 The department of lifelong education, advancement, and potential
3 shall ensure that state scholarships are well publicized and that
4 high school students are provided information on the availability
5 of financial aid. The department of lifelong education,
6 advancement, and potential may receive and expend funds received
7 from outside sources for scholarships, marketing, or other purposes
8 related to Michigan state scholarships. The department of lifelong
9 education, advancement, and potential shall provide the necessary
10 funding and staff to fully operate the programs.supporting state
11 grants and financial aid allocated in section 236(6).
12 (6) For the fiscal year ending September 30, 2026,
13 $300,000,000.00 of ongoing funding is deposited into the
14 postsecondary scholarship fund from the following:
15 (a) State school aid fund, $200,000,000.00.
16 (b) State general fund/general purpose money, $100,000,000.00.

17 Sec. 236m. Funds appropriated in section 236(2) for
18 infrastructure, technology, equipment, maintenance, and safety are
19 intended to be used for necessary improvements and deferred
20 maintenance of public university buildings, facilities, and other
21 physical infrastructure; necessary improvements and deferred
22 maintenance of information technology, other technology
23 infrastructure, and other equipment; and other purposes related to
24 infrastructure, technology, equipment, and maintenance. A public
25 university may also use these funds for debt or to upgrade safety
26 and security infrastructure. These funds are not intended to be
27 used for any other purpose than what is specified in this section.
28 Sec. 236s. (1) Not later than 30 days after the enactment of
29 the amendatory act that added this section, the legislature shall
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) e6cdo0
420
1 provide to the responsible entity and the state budget director a
2 list of legislatively directed spending items, which may be
3 referred to in this section as grants or direct appropriation
4 grants, funded under the amendatory act that added this section
5 consistent with house or senate rules and this section. The list
6 must include all information and documents pertaining to the funded
7 items as publicly disclosed in accordance with house or senate
8 rules and this section.
9 (2) Notwithstanding any other conditions or requirements for
10 direct appropriation grants, the responsible entity shall perform,
11 at a minimum, at least all of the following activities to
12 administer the grants described in subsection (1):
13 (a) Establish a process to review, complete, and execute a
14 grant agreement with a grant recipient. The responsible entity
15 shall not execute a grant agreement unless all necessary
16 documentation has been submitted and reviewed.
17 (b) Verify to the extent possible that a grant recipient is a
18 not-for-profit entity and will use funds as publicly disclosed and
19 for a public purpose that serves the economic prosperity, health,
20 safety, or general welfare of the residents of this state.
21 (c) Review and verify all necessary information to ensure the
22 grant recipient is reasonably able to execute the grant agreement,
23 perform its fiduciary duty, and comply with all applicable state
24 and federal statutes. The responsible entity may deduct the cost of
25 background checks and any other efforts performed as part of this
26 verification from the amount of the designated grant award.
27 (d) Disburse the grant money per the grant disbursement
28 schedule in the executed grant agreement on a reimbursement basis
29 after the grantee has provided sufficient documentation, as
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) e6cdo0
421
1 determined by the responsible entity, to verify that expenditures
2 were made in accordance with the project purpose.
3 (e) If the state budget director determines that information
4 provided by the grantee does not meet the disclosure requirements,
5 that the grant will be used to pay a tax lien, delinquent tax, or
6 other obligation owed to this state, or that the grant will create
7 a conflict of interest, the responsible entity shall not release
8 the grant money to the grantee. Money that is not released under
9 this subdivision lapses at the end of the fiscal year. There is not
10 a conflict of interest if the sponsoring legislator certifies that
11 the sponsoring legislator's immediate family members, legislative
12 staff members that have worked for the sponsoring legislator within
13 the past 2 years, and the sponsoring legislator do not have a
14 direct or indirect pecuniary interest in the legislatively directed
15 spending item.
16 (3) An executed grant agreement under this section between the
17 responsible entity and a grant recipient must include at least all
18 of the following:
19 (a) All necessary identifying information for the grant
20 recipient, including any tax and financial information necessary
21 for the responsible entity to administer grant money under this
22 section.
23 (b) A description of the project for which the grant money
24 will be expended, including tentative timelines and the estimated
25 budget. Project budget must include how all grant money will be
26 used and must indicate if any grant money will be provided to a
27 third party or subrecipient. The responsible entity shall not
28 reimburse expenditures that are outside of the project purpose, as
29 stated in the executed grant agreement, from appropriations under
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) e6cdo0
422
1 the amendatory act that added this section. The grantee shall
2 return to the state treasury any interest in excess of $1,000.00
3 earned on the grant money while unexpended and in possession of the
4 grantee.
5 (c) Unless otherwise specified in the responsible entity's
6 policy, a requirement that funds appropriated for the grants
7 described in subsection (1) may be used only for expenditures that
8 occur on or after the effective date of the amendatory act that
9 added this section.
10 (d) A requirement for reporting by the grant recipient to the

20 Sec. 239a. Public universities are encouraged to evaluate and
21 pursue efficiency and cost-containment measures that maximize state
22 funding. Public universities shall identify practices that increase
23 efficiencies, including, but not limited to, establishing joint
24 ventures, consolidating services, utilizing program collaborations,
25 maximizing educational benefits through optimal class sizes and
26 frequency of course offerings, eliminating low-enrollment and high-
27 cost instructional programs, using self-insurance, practicing
28 energy conservation, and utilizing group purchasing. Public
29 universities shall also review proposed capital outlay projects to

17 academic advisor at least once per semester or term.

19 hours to assist prospective and current students complete in
20 completing the Free Application for Federal Student Aid.
21 (h) The public university posts the costs of degree
22 attainment, separated by degree level, and the earnings associated
23 with degree attainment, separated by degree level.
24 (2) The state budget director shall implement uniform
25 reporting requirements to ensure that a public university receiving
26 a payment under section 236 for operations increase has satisfied
27 the institutional best practices requirements of this section. The
28 state budget director has the sole authority to determine if a
29 public university has met the requirements of this section.
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) e6cdo0
442
1 Information reported by a public university to the state budget
2 director under this subsection must also be reported to the house
3 and senate appropriations subcommittees on higher education and the
4 house and senate fiscal agencies.standard report recipients.
5 (3) If a public university fails to comply with the
6 certification requirements of this section, the state treasurer may
7 withhold the monthly installments under section 241 to the public
8 university until the report is submitted. If a public university
9 does not comply with the certification requirements described in
10 this section by the end of the fiscal year, the public university
11 forfeits the amount withheld. Forfeited funds must lapse to the
12 fund from which the funds were appropriated. The state budget
13 director shall notify the chairs of the house and senate
14 appropriations subcommittees on higher education standard report
15 recipients at least 10 days before withholding funds from any
16 public university.
17 Sec. 241h. It is the intent of the legislature that taxpayer
18 Taxpayer funds appropriated under this article are to be used to
19 educate public university students and continue maintenance of
20 public university buildings and other assets. The goal of the
21 legislature is that taxpayer funds not be used excessively for
22 administration. Therefore, a public university that receives an
23 appropriation under this article shall not spend more than 10% of
24 that appropriation on administration. A sum of money spent in
25 excess of the 10% cap described in this section by a public
26 university results in a forfeiture of an amount equal to 50% of
27 that sum from funds appropriated to that public university, not to
28 exceed the total amount appropriated to that public university
29 under this article. The auditor general may investigate allegations
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) e6cdo0
443
1 of violations of this section. Funds forfeited under this section
2 must revert, to the extent permitted by law, to the general fund or
3 the state school aid fund. For the purpose of calculations under
4 this section, the amount spent by a public university on
5 administrative costs does not include costs associated with staff
6 working in the following roles: teachers, facilities, public
7 safety, technology, research, instruction, academic support,
8 student services, auxiliary services, or public service.

26 (2) As used in this section:
27 (a) "Cost of attendance" means expenses for a student's

29 program of study; books, supplies, and equipment required for

1 courses of instruction; housing and food costs; transportation
2 expenses; federal student loan fees; miscellaneous expenses,

6 license, certification, or first professional credential; and
7 reasonable costs for study abroad programs.

9 advancement, and potential.
10 (c) "Eligible institution" means a public university that
11 receives an appropriation in section 236, a community college that
12 receives an appropriation in section 201, a federally recognized
13 tribal college in this state, or an independent nonprofit college
14 or university in this state as described in section 1 of 1966 PA
15 313, MCL 390.991.
16 (d) "Gift aid" includes federal Pell grants under 20 USC
17 1070a, tuition incentive program benefits under section 256, state
18 tuition grants under section 252, awards received for minimum
19 payments awarded in subsection (4), higher education expenses paid
20 under the Michigan promise zone authority act, 2008 PA 549, MCL
21 390.1661 to 390.1679, and all other federal, state, local, or
22 institutional aid in the form of grants, scholarships, or discounts
23 applied toward tuition and mandatory fees. Gift aid does not
24 include student loans, work-study awards, qualified withdrawals
25 made from education savings accounts to pay higher education
26 expenses pursuant to the Michigan education savings program act,
27 2000 PA 161, MCL 390.1471 to 390.1486, or higher education expenses
28 paid under the Michigan education trust program pursuant to the
29 Michigan education trust act, 1986 PA 316, MCL 390.1421 to

1 390.1442.
2 (e) "High school equivalency certificate" means that term as

6 recognized tribal college, an amount equal to the student's
7 tuition, mandatory fees, and contact hours for the student's actual
8 program of study, minus all gift aid received by the student.
9 (ii) For a student attending a public university or an
10 independent nonprofit college or university, or for a student

17 payment must be awarded as a separate payment not included in the
18 student's need-based financial aid. The minimum payment must not be
19 reduced.

27 Sec. 256. (1) The funds appropriated in section 236 for the
28 tuition incentive program must be distributed as provided in this
29 section and pursuant to the administrative procedures for the
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) e6cdo0
457
1 tuition incentive program of the department of lifelong education,
2 advancement, and potential.
3 (2) As used in this section:
4 (a) "Phase I" means the first part of the tuition incentive
5 program defined as the academic period of 80 semester or 120 term
6 credits, or less, leading to an associate degree or certificate.
7 Students must be enrolled in a certificate or associate degree
8 program and taking classes within the program of study for a
9 certificate or associate degree. Tuition will not be covered for
10 courses outside of a certificate or associate degree program.
11 (b) "Phase II" means the second part of the tuition incentive
12 program that provides assistance in the third and fourth year of 4-
13 year degree programs.
14 (c) "Department" means the department of lifelong education,
15 advancement, and potential.
16 (d) "High school equivalency certificate" means that term as
17 defined in section 4.
18 (3) An individual must meet the following basic criteria and
19 financial thresholds to be eligible for tuition incentive program
20 benefits:
21 (a) To be eligible for phase I, an individual must meet all of
22 the following criteria:
23 (i) Be less than 20 years of age at the time the individual
24 graduates from high school with a diploma or certificate of
25 completion or achieves a high school equivalency certificate or,
26 for students attending a 5-year middle college approved by the
27 Michigan department of education, be less than 21 years of age when
28 the individual graduates from high school, or be considered a
29 special education pupil at the time the individual graduated from
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) e6cdo0
458
1 high school as described in section 6(4)(l)(i).
2 (ii) Be a United States citizen and maintain residency in this
3 state, as determined for purposes of the Free Application for
4 Federal Student Aid.
5 (iii) Be at least a half-time student, earning less than 80
6 semester or 120 term credits at a participating educational
7 institution within 4 years of high school graduation or achievement
8 of a high school equivalency certificate. All program eligibility
9 expires 10 years after initial enrollment at a participating
10 educational institution.
11 (iv) Meet the satisfactory academic progress policy of the
12 educational institution attended by the individual.
13 (b) To be eligible for phase II, an individual must meet
14 either of the following criteria in addition to the criteria in
15 subdivision (a):
16 (i) Complete at least 56 transferable semester or 84
17 transferable term credits.
18 (ii) Obtain an associate degree or certificate at a
19 participating institution.
20 (c) To be eligible for phase I or phase II, an individual must
21 be financially eligible as determined by the department. An
22 individual is financially eligible for the tuition incentive
23 program if the individual was eligible for Medicaid from this state
24 for 24 months within any 36 months prior to completion of high
25 school or achievement of a high school equivalency certificate. The
26 department shall accept certification of Medicaid eligibility only
27 from the department of health and human services for the purposes
28 of verifying if a person is Medicaid eligible for 24 months within
29 any 36 months prior to completion of high school or achievement of
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) e6cdo0
459
1 a high school equivalency certificate. Certification of eligibility
2 may begin in the sixth grade.
3 (4) For phase I, the department shall provide payment on
4 behalf of a person eligible under subsection (3). The department
5 shall only accept standard per-credit hour tuition billings and
6 shall reject billings that are excessive or outside the guidelines
7 for the type of educational institution.
8 (5) For phase I, all of the following apply:
9 (a) Payments for associate degree or certificate programs must
10 not be made for more than 80 semester or 120 term credits for any
11 individual student at any participating institution. The department
12 shall not do either of the following:
13 (i) Adopt or apply any total semester-credit or term-credit
14 maximum that is less than the 80 semester-credit or 120 term-credit
15 maximum provided in this subdivision.
16 (ii) Adopt or apply any per-semester or per-term credit maximum
17 for a student whose semester-credit or term-credit load will not
18 result in exceeding the total 80 semester-credit or 120 term-credit
19 maximum provided in this subdivision.
20 (b) For persons enrolled at a Michigan community college, the
21 department shall pay the current in-district tuition and mandatory
22 fees. For persons residing in an area that is not included in any
23 community college district, the out-of-district tuition rate may be
24 authorized.
25 (c) For persons enrolled at a Michigan public university, the
26 department shall pay lower division resident tuition and mandatory
27 fees for the current year.
28 (d) For persons enrolled at a Michigan independent, nonprofit
29 degree-granting college or university or a Michigan federal
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) e6cdo0
460
1 tribally controlled community college, the department shall pay
2 mandatory fees for the current year and a per-credit payment that
3 does not exceed the average community college in-district per-
4 credit tuition rate as reported by the last business day of August
5 for the immediately preceding academic year.
6 (6) A person participating in phase II may be eligible for
7 additional funds not to exceed $500.00 per semester or $400.00 per
8 term up to a maximum of $2,000.00 subject to the following
9 conditions:
10 (a) Credits are earned in a 4-year program at a Michigan
11 degree-granting 4-year college or university.
12 (b) The tuition reimbursement is for coursework completed
13 within 30 months of completion of the phase I requirements.
14 (7) The department shall work closely with participating
15 institutions to provide the highest level of participation and
16 ensure that all requirements of the program are met.
17 (8) The department shall notify students in an age-appropriate
18 manner of their financial eligibility for the program any time
19 after the student begins sixth grade.
20 (9) Except as otherwise provided in sections 13(c) and 17 of
21 the Michigan reconnect grant recipient act, 2020 PA 68, MCL
22 390.1713 and 390.1717, each institution shall ensure that all known
23 available restricted grants for tuition and fees are used before
24 billing the tuition incentive program for any portion of a
25 student's tuition and fees.
26 (10) The department shall ensure that the tuition incentive
27 program is well publicized and that eligible Medicaid clients are
28 provided information on the program. The department shall provide
29 the necessary funding and staff to fully operate the program.

1 (11) The department shall collaborate with the center to use
2 the P-20 longitudinal data system to report the following

24 Sec. 260. (1) The department of lifelong education,
25 advancement, and potential shall work with student and
26 postsecondary education groups, including the Michigan College
27 Access Network, the Michigan Association of School Counselors, the
28 Michigan Association of State Universities, the Michigan Community
29 College Association, and the Michigan Independent Colleges and
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) e6cdo0
462
1 Universities, to provide and update an online informational
2 resource for students in grades 9 through 12 and prospective and
3 current students and families. The online informational resource
4 must be a website or a portion of an existing website titled
5 "Paying for College in Michigan" and designed and maintained by the
6 department of lifelong education, advancement, and potential that,
7 to the extent practicable, contains information, including, but not
8 limited to, all of the following:
9 (a) A list of public and private community support centers,
10 student debt clinics, and other organizations and their contact
11 information submitted by Michigan College Access Network that
12 provides free information and services for student loan borrowers
13 to help educate them about repayment options and to help them
14 access student loan programs or benefits for which they may be
15 eligible.
16 (b) Links to state and federal financial aid programs,
17 including FAFSA and College Scorecard.
18 (c) Links to each promise zone website and the financial aid
19 website to each community college, public university, and
20 independent college and university in this state.
21 (d) Benefits of federal student loans that may no longer be
22 available if a borrower refinances a loan.
23 (e) Direct links to net price calculators for each community
24 college receiving an appropriation in section 201 and each public
25 university receiving an appropriation in section 236.
26 (f) Definitions that clearly delineate the differences between
27 scholarships, grants, and loans.
28 (g) A description of net price calculators and how to use them
29 to create a personalized estimate of a student's out-of-pocket cost
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) e6cdo0
463
1 for the coming year based on basic family and financial information
2 and likely financial aid eligibility.
3 (h) Information on the fundamentals of borrowing and
4 repayment, including, but not limited to, all of the following:
5 (i) A link to the federal Public Service Loan Forgiveness
6 Program or other state or federal loan forgiveness programs.
7 (ii) Deciding how much to borrow.
8 (iii) Creating a plan for borrowing and repayment.
9 (iv) Estimating how much borrowing is needed for a given school
10 year.
11 (v) Factors that affect total student loan costs.
12 (vi) Tips for graduating with less student loan debt.
13 (vii) A loan payment calculator or a link to a loan payment

15 (viii) Links to federal student loan entrance and exit
16 counseling services and the FACT tool.
17 (ix) Student loan debt relief scams.
18 (i) Loan amortization information.
19 (j) The costs of degree attainment, separated by degree level,
