THE PEOPLE OF THE STATE OF MICHIGAN ENACT:
1 Sec. 6. (1) "Center program" means a program operated by a
2 district or by an intermediate district for special education
3 pupils from several districts in programs for pupils with autism
4 spectrum disorder, pupils with severe cognitive impairment, pupils
5 with moderate cognitive impairment, pupils with severe multiple
6 impairments, pupils with hearing impairment, pupils with visual
7 impairment, and pupils with physical impairment or other health
8 impairment. Programs for pupils with emotional impairment housed in
9 buildings that do not serve regular education pupils also qualify.
10 Unless otherwise approved by the department, a center program
11 either serves all constituent districts within an intermediate
12 district or serves several districts with less than 50% of the
13 pupils residing in the operating district. In addition, special
14 education center program pupils placed part-time in noncenter
15 programs to comply with the least restrictive environment
16 provisions of section 1412 of the individuals with disabilities
17 education act, 20 USC 1412, may be considered center program pupils
18 for pupil accounting purposes for the time scheduled in either a
19 center program or a noncenter program.
20 (2) "District and high school graduation rate" means the
21 annual completion and pupil dropout rate that is calculated by the
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) e6cdo0

4
1 center pursuant to nationally recognized standards.
2 (3) "District and high school graduation report" means a
3 report of the number of pupils, excluding adult education
4 participants, in the district for the immediately preceding school
5 year, adjusted for those pupils who have transferred into or out of
6 the district or high school, who leave high school with a diploma
7 or other credential of equal status.
8 (4) "Membership", except as otherwise provided in this
9 article, means for a district, a public school academy, or an
10 intermediate district the sum of the product of .90 times the
11 number of full-time equated pupils in grades K to 12 actually
12 enrolled and in regular daily attendance in the district, public
13 school academy, or intermediate district on the pupil membership
14 count day for the current school year, plus the product of .10
15 times the final audited count from the supplemental count day of
16 full-time equated pupils in grades K to 12 actually enrolled and in
17 regular daily attendance in the district, public school academy, or
18 intermediate district for the immediately preceding school year. A
19 district's, public school academy's, or intermediate district's
20 membership is adjusted as provided under section 25e for pupils who
21 enroll after the pupil membership count day in a strict discipline
22 academy operating under sections 1311b to 1311m of the revised
23 school code, MCL 380.1311b to 380.1311m. All pupil counts used in
24 this subsection are as determined by the department and calculated
25 by adding the number of pupils registered for attendance plus
26 pupils received by transfer and minus pupils lost as defined by
27 rules promulgated by the superintendent, and as corrected by a
28 subsequent department audit. The amount of the foundation allowance
29 for a pupil in membership is determined under section 20. In making
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) e6cdo0

5
1 the calculation of membership, all of the following, as applicable,
2 apply to determining the membership of a district, a public school
3 academy, or an intermediate district:
4 (a) Except as otherwise provided in this subsection, and
5 pursuant to subsection (6), a pupil is counted in membership in the
6 pupil's educating district or districts. Except as otherwise
7 provided in this subsection, an individual pupil must not be
8 counted for more than a total of 1.0 full-time equated membership.
9 (b) If a pupil is educated in a district other than the
10 pupil's district of residence, if the pupil is not being educated
11 as part of a cooperative education program, if the pupil's district
12 of residence does not give the educating district its approval to
13 count the pupil in membership in the educating district, and if the
14 pupil is not covered by an exception specified in subsection (6) to
15 the requirement that the educating district must have the approval
16 of the pupil's district of residence to count the pupil in
17 membership, the pupil is not counted in membership in any district.
18 (c) A special education pupil educated by the intermediate
19 district is counted in membership in the intermediate district.
20 (d) A pupil placed by a court or state agency in an on-grounds
21 program of a juvenile detention facility, a child caring
22 institution, or a mental health institution, or a pupil funded
23 under section 53a, is counted in membership in the district or
24 intermediate district approved by the department to operate the
25 program.
26 (e) A pupil enrolled in the Michigan Schools for the Deaf and
27 Blind is counted in membership in the pupil's intermediate district
28 of residence.
29 (f) A pupil enrolled in a career and technical education
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) e6cdo0

6
1 program supported by a millage levied over an area larger than a
2 single district or in an area vocational-technical education
3 program established under section 690 of the revised school code,
4 MCL 380.690, is counted in membership only in the pupil's district
5 of residence.
6 (g) A pupil enrolled in a public school academy is counted in
7 membership in the public school academy.
8 (h) For the purposes of this section and section 6a, for a
9 cyber school, as that term is defined in section 551 of the revised
10 school code, MCL 380.551, that is in compliance with section 553a
11 of the revised school code, MCL 380.553a, a pupil's participation
12 in the cyber school's educational program is considered regular
13 daily attendance, and for a district or public school academy, a
14 pupil's participation in a virtual course as that term is defined
15 in section 21f is considered regular daily attendance. Beginning
16 July 1, 2021, this subdivision is subject to section 8c. It is the
17 intent of the legislature that the immediately preceding sentence
18 apply retroactively and is effective July 1, 2021. For the purposes
19 of this subdivision, for a pupil enrolled in a cyber school, all of
20 the following apply with regard to the participation requirement as
21 described in this subdivision:
22 (i) Except as otherwise provided in this subdivision, the pupil
23 shall participate in each scheduled course on pupil membership
24 count day or supplemental count day, as applicable. If the pupil is
25 absent on pupil membership count day or supplemental count day, as
26 applicable, the pupil must attend and participate in class during
27 the next 10 consecutive school days if the absence was unexcused,
28 or during the next 30 calendar days if the absence was excused.
29 (ii) For a pupil who is not learning sequentially, 1 or more of
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) e6cdo0

7
1 the following must be met on pupil membership count day or
2 supplemental count day, as applicable, for each scheduled course to
3 satisfy the participation requirement under this subdivision:
4 (A) The pupil attended a live lesson from the teacher.
5 (B) The pupil logged into a lesson or lesson activity and the
6 login can be documented.
7 (C) The pupil and teacher engaged in a subject-oriented
8 telephone conversation.
9 (D) There is documentation of an email dialogue between the
10 pupil and teacher.
11 (E) There is documentation of activity or work between the
12 learning coach and pupil.
13 (F) An alternate form of attendance as determined and agreed
14 upon by the cyber school and the pupil membership auditor was met.
15 (iii) For a pupil using sequential learning, the participation
16 requirement under this subdivision is satisfied if either of the
17 following occurs:
18 (A) Except as otherwise provided in this sub-subparagraph, the
19 pupil and the teacher of record or mentor complete a 2-way
20 interaction for 1 course during the week on which pupil membership
21 count day or supplemental count day, as applicable, occurs, and the
22 3 consecutive weeks following that week. However, if a school break
23 is scheduled during any of the weeks described in this sub-
24 subparagraph that is 4 or more days in length or instruction has
25 been canceled districtwide during any of the weeks described in
26 this sub-subparagraph for 3 or more school days, the district is
27 not required to ensure that the pupil and the teacher of record or
28 mentor completed a 2-way interaction for that week. As used in this
29 sub-subparagraph:
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) e6cdo0

8
1 (I) "2-way interaction" means the communication that occurs
2 between the teacher of record or mentor and pupil, where 1 party
3 initiates communication and a response from the other party follows
4 that communication. Responses as described in this sub-sub-
5 subparagraph must be to the communication initiated by the teacher
6 of record or mentor, and not some other action taken. This
7 interaction may occur through, but is not limited to, means such as
8 email, telephone, instant messaging, or face-to-face conversation.
9 A parent- or legal-guardian-facilitated 2-way interaction is
10 considered a 2-way interaction if the pupil is in any of grades K
11 to 5 and does not yet possess the skills necessary to participate
12 in 2-way interactions unassisted. The interactions described in
13 this sub-sub-subparagraph must relate to a virtual course on the
14 pupil's schedule and pertain to course content or progress.
15 (II) "Mentor" means a professional employee of the district
16 who monitors the pupil's progress, ensures the pupil has access to
17 needed technology, is available for assistance, and ensures access
18 to the teacher of record. A mentor may also be the teacher of
19 record if the mentor meets the definition of a teacher of record
20 under this sub-subparagraph and the district is the provider for
21 the course.
22 (III) "Teacher of record" means a teacher to whom all of the
23 following apply:
24 (1) The teacher is responsible for providing instruction,
25 determining instructional methods for each pupil, diagnosing
26 learning needs, assessing pupil learning, prescribing intervention
27 strategies and modifying lessons, reporting outcomes, and
28 evaluating the effects of instruction and support strategies. The
29 teacher of record may coordinate the distribution and assignment of
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) e6cdo0

9
1 the responsibilities described in this sub-sub-sub-subparagraph
2 with other teachers participating in the instructional process for
3 a course.
4 (2) The teacher is certified for the grade level or is working
5 under a valid substitute permit, authorization, or approval issued
6 by the department.
7 (3) The teacher has a personnel identification code provided
8 by the center.
9 (IV) "Week" means a period that starts on Wednesday and ends
10 the following Tuesday.
11 (B) The pupil completes a combination of 1 or more of the
12 following activities for each scheduled course on pupil membership
13 count day or supplemental count day, as applicable:
14 (I) Documented attendance in a virtual course where
15 synchronous, live instruction occurred with the teacher.
16 (II) Documented completion of a course assignment.
17 (III) Documented completion of a course lesson or lesson
18 activity.
19 (IV) Documented pupil access to an ongoing lesson, which does
20 not include a login.
21 (V) Documented physical attendance on pupil membership count
22 day or supplemental count day, as applicable, in each scheduled
23 course, if the pupil will attend at least 50% of the instructional
24 time for each scheduled course on-site, face-to-face with the
25 teacher of record. As used in this sub-sub-subparagraph, "teacher
26 of record" means that term as defined in subparagraph (iii)(A).
27 (iv) For purposes of subparagraph (iii), each scheduled course
28 currently being attempted by the pupil, rather than every course on
29 the pupil's schedule for the entire term, is considered a part of
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) e6cdo0

10
1 each scheduled course for the pupil.
2 (i) For a new district or public school academy beginning its
3 operation after December 31, 1994, membership for the first 2 full
4 or partial fiscal years of operation is determined as follows:
5 (i) If operations begin before the pupil membership count day
6 for the fiscal year, membership is the average number of full-time
7 equated pupils in grades K to 12 actually enrolled and in regular
8 daily attendance on the pupil membership count day for the current
9 school year and on the supplemental count day for the current
10 school year, as determined by the department and calculated by
11 adding the number of pupils registered for attendance on the pupil
12 membership count day plus pupils received by transfer and minus
13 pupils lost as defined by rules promulgated by the superintendent,
14 and as corrected by a subsequent department audit, plus the final
15 audited count from the supplemental count day for the current
16 school year, and dividing that sum by 2.
17 (ii) If operations begin after the pupil membership count day
18 for the fiscal year and not later than the supplemental count day
19 for the fiscal year, membership is the final audited count of the
20 number of full-time equated pupils in grades K to 12 actually
21 enrolled and in regular daily attendance on the supplemental count
22 day for the current school year.
23 (j) If a district is the authorizing body for a public school

6 counted in membership.
7 (l) To be counted in membership, a pupil must meet the minimum
8 age requirement to be eligible to attend school under section 1147

10 years of age on September 1 of the school year except as follows:

18 degree in fewer than 5 years. A pupil who graduates with both a
19 high school diploma and at least 60 transferable college credits or
20 an associate degree at least 1 semester early is considered to have
21 completed the middle college program in fewer than 5 years.
22 (ll) If a district's or public school academy's membership for
23 a particular fiscal year, as otherwise calculated under this
24 subsection, includes pupils counted in membership who are enrolled
25 under section 166b, all of the following apply for the purposes of
26 this subdivision:
27 (i) If the district's or public school academy's membership for
28 pupils counted under section 166b equals or exceeds 5% of the
29 district's or public school academy's membership for pupils not
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) e6cdo0

23
1 counted in membership under section 166b in the immediately
2 preceding fiscal year, then the growth in the district's or public
3 school academy's membership for pupils counted under section 166b
4 must not exceed 10%.
5 (ii) If the district's or public school academy's membership
6 for pupils counted under section 166b is less than 5% of the
7 district's or public school academy's membership for pupils not
8 counted in membership under section 166b in the immediately
9 preceding fiscal year, then the district's or public school
10 academy's membership for pupils counted under section 166b must not
11 exceed the greater of the following:
12 (A) Five percent of the district's or public school academy's
13 membership for pupils not counted in membership under section 166b.
14 (B) Ten percent more than the district's or public school
15 academy's membership for pupils counted under section 166b in the
16 immediately preceding fiscal year.
17 (iii) If 1 or more districts consolidate or are parties to an
18 annexation, then the calculations under subparagraphs (i) and (ii)
19 must be applied to the combined total membership for pupils counted
20 in those districts for the fiscal year immediately preceding the
21 consolidation or annexation.
22 (mm) A pupil that enrolls in a district after the pupil
23 membership count day may be counted as 1/11 of a full-time equated
24 pupil for each month the pupil is enrolled in the district between
25 October and August of the current fiscal year.
26 (5) "Public school academy" means that term as defined in
27 section 5 of the revised school code, MCL 380.5.
28 (6) "Pupil" means an individual in membership in a public
29 school. A district must have the approval of the pupil's district
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) e6cdo0

24
1 of residence to count the pupil in membership, except approval by
2 the pupil's district of residence is not required for any of the
3 following:
4 (a) A nonpublic part-time pupil enrolled in grades K to 12 in
5 accordance with section 166b.
6 (b) A pupil receiving 1/2 or less of the pupil's instruction
7 in a district other than the pupil's district of residence.
8 (c) A pupil enrolled in a public school academy.
9 (d) A pupil enrolled in a district other than the pupil's
10 district of residence if the pupil is enrolled in accordance with
11 section 105 or 105c.
12 (e) A pupil who has made an official written complaint or
13 whose parent or legal guardian has made an official written
14 complaint to law enforcement officials and to school officials of
15 the pupil's district of residence that the pupil has been the
16 victim of a criminal sexual assault or other serious assault, if
17 the official complaint either indicates that the assault occurred
18 at school or that the assault was committed by 1 or more other
19 pupils enrolled in the school the pupil would otherwise attend in
20 the district of residence or by an employee of the district of
21 residence. A person who intentionally makes a false report of a
22 crime to law enforcement officials for the purposes of this
23 subdivision is subject to section 411a of the Michigan penal code,
24 1931 PA 328, MCL 750.411a, which provides criminal penalties for
25 that conduct. As used in this subdivision:
26 (i) "At school" means in a classroom, elsewhere on school
27 premises, on a school bus or other school-related vehicle, or at a
28 school-sponsored activity or event whether or not it is held on
29 school premises.
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) e6cdo0

25
1 (ii) "Serious assault" means an act that constitutes a felony
2 violation of chapter XI of the Michigan penal code, 1931 PA 328,
3 MCL 750.81 to 750.90h, 750.90g, or that constitutes an assault and
4 infliction of serious or aggravated injury under section 81a of the
5 Michigan penal code, 1931 PA 328, MCL 750.81a.
6 (f) A pupil whose district of residence changed after the
7 pupil membership count day and before the supplemental count day
8 and who continues to be enrolled on the supplemental count day as a
9 nonresident in the district in which the pupil was enrolled as a
10 resident on the pupil membership count day of the same school year.
11 (g) A pupil enrolled in an alternative education program
12 operated by a district other than the pupil's district of residence
13 who meets 1 or more of the following:
14 (i) The pupil has been suspended or expelled from the pupil's
15 district of residence for any reason, including, but not limited
16 to, a suspension or expulsion under section 1310, 1311, or 1311a of
17 the revised school code, MCL 380.1310, 380.1311, and 380.1311a.
18 (ii) The pupil had previously dropped out of school.
19 (iii) The pupil is pregnant or is a parent.
20 (iv) The pupil has been referred to the program by a court.
21 (h) A pupil enrolled in the Michigan Virtual School, for the
22 pupil's enrollment in the Michigan Virtual School.
23 (i) A pupil who is the child of a person who works at the
24 district or who is the child of a person who worked at the district
25 as of the time the pupil first enrolled in the district but who no
26 longer works at the district due to a workforce reduction. As used
27 in this subdivision, "child" includes an adopted child, stepchild,
28 or legal ward.

CRM H06167'26 (H-1) e6cdo0

1 expelling district and is reinstated by another school board under

22 educating district must have the approval of that other district to

27
1 session on the Wednesday described in subparagraph (i) due to

9 (ii) First Wednesday in October.
10 (iii) Second Wednesday in February.

13 religious or cultural significance, as determined by the district

16 (8) "Pupils in grades K to 12 actually enrolled and in regular

20 membership count day or the supplemental count day, as applicable.
21 Except as otherwise provided in this section and subsection, a

1 state treasurer shall deposit into the school aid stabilization

5 Sec. 16a. (1) The curriculum used by a district must not, in
6 any way, include the promotion of any form of race or gender
7 stereotyping or anything that could be understood as implicit race
8 or gender stereotyping. As used in this subsection, "race or gender
9 stereotyping" means a set of statements, beliefs, or ideas that
10 conform wholly or in part with the following general or particular
11 statements:
12 (a) That all individuals comprising a racial or ethnic group
13 or gender hold a collective quality or belief.
14 (b) That individuals act in certain ways or hold certain
15 opinions because of their race or gender.
16 (c) That individuals are born racist or sexist by accident of
17 their race or gender.
18 (d) That individuals bear collective guilt for historical
19 wrongs committed by their race or gender.
20 (e) That cultural norms or practices of a racial or ethnic
21 group or gender are flawed and must be eliminated or changed to
22 conform with those of another racial or ethnic group or gender.
23 (f) That racism is inherent in individuals from a particular
24 race or ethnic group or that sexism is inherent in individuals from
25 a particular gender.
26 (g) That a racial or ethnic group or gender is in need of
27 deconstruction, elimination, or criticism.
28 (h) That the actions of individuals serve as an indictment
29 against the race or gender of those individuals.

15 (14) As used in this section:
16 (a) "Certified mills" means the lesser of 18 mills or the
17 number of mills of school operating taxes levied by the district in
18 1993-94.
19 (b) "Current fiscal year" means the fiscal year for which a
20 particular calculation is made.

22 organization, has its territory attached to 1 or more other
23 districts, and is dissolved as provided under section 12 of the
24 revised school code, MCL 380.12.

26 immediately preceding the current fiscal year.

29 the product of the taxable value per membership pupil of all
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) e6cdo0

59
1 property in the district that is nonexempt property times the
2 district's certified mills and, for a district with certified mills
3 exceeding 12, the product of the taxable value per membership pupil
4 of property in the district that is commercial personal property
5 times the certified mills minus 12 mills) and (the quotient of the
6 product of the captured assessed valuation under tax increment
7 financing acts times the district's certified mills divided by the
8 district's membership excluding special education pupils).
9 (f) "Membership" means the definition of that term under
10 section 6 as in effect for the particular fiscal year for which a
11 particular calculation is made.
12 (g) "Nonexempt property" means property that is not a
13 principal residence, qualified agricultural property, qualified
14 forest property, supportive housing property, industrial personal
15 property, commercial personal property, or property occupied by a
16 public school academy.

65
1 considered for assignment to a partnership district agreement. The
2 purpose of the partnership district agreement is to identify
3 district needs, develop intervention plans, and partner with
4 public, private, and nonprofit organizations to coordinate
5 resources and improve student achievement. Assignment of a district
6 to a partnership district agreement is made by the superintendent.
7 (2) A district described in subsection (1) is eligible for
8 funding under this section if the district includes at least 1
9 school that has been identified as low performing under the
10 approved federal accountability system. A district described in
11 this subsection must do all of the following to be eligible for
12 funding under this section:
13 (a) Within 90 days of assignment to the partnership district
14 agreement described in this section, complete a comprehensive needs
15 assessment or evaluation in collaboration with an intermediate
16 district, community members, education organizations, and
17 postsecondary institutions, as applicable, that is approved by the
18 superintendent. The comprehensive needs assessment or evaluation
19 must include at least all of the following:
20 (i) A review of the district's implementation and utilization
21 of a multi-tiered system of supports to ensure that it is used to
22 appropriately inform instruction.
23 (ii) A review of the district and school building leadership
24 and educator capacity to substantially improve student outcomes.
25 (iii) A review of classroom, instructional, and operational
26 practices and curriculum to ensure alignment with research-based
27 instructional practices and state curriculum standards.
28 (b) Develop a district continuous improvement plan that has
29 been approved by the superintendent and that addresses the needs
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) e6cdo0

66
1 identified in the comprehensive needs assessment or evaluation
2 completed under subdivision (a). The district continuous
3 improvement plan must include at least all of the following:
4 (i) Specific actions that will be taken by the district and
5 each of its partners to improve student achievement.
6 (ii) Specific measurable benchmarks that will be met within 18
7 months to improve student achievement and identification of
8 expected student achievement outcomes to be attained within 3 years
9 after assignment to the partnership.
10 (iii) Measurable benchmarks that put pupils on track to meet or
11 exceed grade level proficiency, increase high school graduation
12 rates, reduce class sizes in grades K to 3, and improve attendance
13 rates.

15 including, but not limited to, the superintendent or chief
16 administrator and school board or board of directors members, on

22 supplemental, or intervention reading approach and to determine why
23 some students struggle with reading, writing, spelling, and
24 language.

26 effective reading and spelling instruction supported by

28 instructional sequence that uses techniques to support teachers'
29 independence in using their newly learned skills with students in
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) e6cdo0

103
1 the classroom.
2 (iii) Integrated components for educators in grades K to 5 with
3 embedded evaluation or assessment of knowledge. Evaluation or

5 evaluations of learning throughout each unit and include a
6 summative assessment that must be completed to demonstrate
7 successful course completion.
8 (iv) Instruction that builds teacher content knowledge and
9 pedagogical knowledge of the critical components of literacy,
10 including how the brain learns to read, phonological and phonemic
11 awareness, letter knowledge, phonics, advanced phonics, vocabulary
12 and oral language, fluency, comprehension, spelling and writing,
13 and the organization of language.

17 intervention for reading difficulties in a multi-tiered system of

19 all of the following essential components:
20 (A) Team-based leadership.
21 (B) A tiered delivery system.
22 (C) Selection and implementation of instruction,
23 interventions, and supports.
24 (D) A comprehensive screening and assessment system.
25 (E) Continuous data-based decision making.

16 Sec. 27o. (1) From the state school aid fund money
17 appropriated in section 11, there is allocated $4,000,000.00 for
18 2026-2027 only to an intermediate district to support a statewide
19 digital credentialing initiative for students in CTE programs and
20 educators. The digital credentialing initiative must provide pupils
21 and instructional staff with access to a verifiable, portable, and
22 accessible digital record of skills, competencies, and achievements
23 that may be securely shared with employers, postsecondary
24 institutions, and workforce partners across this state, supporting
25 Michigan's economic workforce competitiveness by improving skills
26 transparency and portability statewide.
27 (2) Funds received under this section must be awarded to an
28 intermediate district to partner with a nonprofit organization with
29 demonstrated experience in digital credentialing and workforce-
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) e6cdo0

111
1 aligned talent record systems.
2 (3) The digital credentialing initiative must enable CTE
3 students statewide, including program completers and individuals
4 demonstrating proficiency in Perkins V-aligned competencies, to
5 earn verified, portable digital credentials documenting career
6 pathway achievements, workforce readiness, and postsecondary
7 readiness. Credentials shall be aligned to department-approved CTE
8 programs and statewide competency frameworks to ensure consistency
9 and portability across regions.
10 (4) The digital credentialing initiative must include, at a
11 minimum, both of the following:
12 (a) Development of digital credentials for CTE program
13 completion, work-based learning, youth apprenticeship, and soft
14 skills. All credentials must be stored, including industry-
15 recognized credits. Credentials must be maintained within a
16 statewide credential library to support consistent issuance,
17 validation, and recognition across this state. These achievements
18 must be showcased in a digital, portable portfolio that organizes
19 and displays verified credentials, skills, competencies, and
20 achievements and can be securely shared.
21 (b) Digital credentials for educators that track literacy
22 professional development for educators, including the completion of
23 LETRS training or other science of reading-based training.
24 Credentials issued for the completion of LETRS training must be
25 competency based, verifiable, and electronically shareable by the
26 educator. Each credential must document the specific level or unit
27 completed and the date of completion.
28 (5) The development of digital credentials for CTE program
29 completion described in subsection (4)(a) must align with state and
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) e6cdo0

112
1 regional workforce and education systems to support Michigan's
2 economic workforce competitiveness and regional talent pipeline
3 needs, while ensuring statewide consistency and portability of
4 credentials. The administering nonprofit organization shall provide
5 statewide implementation support and submit an annual report to the
6 department detailing adoption, credential issuance by type and
7 region, and progress toward improving statewide CTE-to-workforce
8 outcomes.
9 (6) The administering nonprofit organization shall provide
10 participant level data in a form and manner prescribed by the
11 department to support credential issuance, registry reporting, and
12 integration with statewide education data systems. Data submitted
13 under this section must align with statewide education data
14 infrastructure standards, including coordination with MiDataHub and

17 (4) Each district or public school academy must be assigned an
18 opportunity index score each fiscal year, the value of which is the
19 quotient of the number of economically disadvantaged pupils as
20 determined under subsection (3) for the district or public school
21 academy and the total number of pupils in the district or public
22 school academy in the immediately preceding fiscal year, multiplied
23 by 100 and rounded up to the nearest whole number. Each district or
24 public school academy must be assigned an opportunity index band as
25 follows:
26 (a) A district or public school academy with an opportunity
27 index score greater than or equal to 0 but less than 20 must be
28 assigned to band 1 and shall receive reimbursement under subsection
29 (3) at a rate of at least 35.0% and less than 36.0%. The
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) e6cdo0

123
1 reimbursement rate under this subdivision must be an amount equal
2 to the district's opportunity index score minus 1, multiplied by
3 the band adjustment factor applicable to this subdivision, plus
4 35.0%.
5 (b) A district or public school academy with an opportunity
6 index score greater than or equal to 20 but less than 44 must be
7 assigned to band 2 and shall receive reimbursement under subsection
8 (3) at a rate of at least 36.0% and less than 37.5%. The
9 reimbursement rate under this subdivision must be an amount equal
10 to the district's opportunity index score minus 20, multiplied by
11 the band adjustment factor applicable to this subdivision, plus
12 36.0%.
13 (c) A district or public school academy with an opportunity
14 index score greater than or equal to 44 but less than 59 must be
15 assigned to band 3 and shall receive reimbursement under subsection
16 (3) at a rate of at least 37.5% and less than 39.0%. The
17 reimbursement rate under this subdivision must be an amount equal
18 to the district's opportunity index score minus 44, multiplied by
19 the band adjustment factor applicable to this subdivision, plus
20 37.5%.
21 (d) A district or public school academy with an opportunity
22 index score greater than or equal to 59 but less than 73 must be
23 assigned to band 4 and shall receive reimbursement under subsection
24 (3) at a rate of at least 39.0% and less than 42.0%. The
25 reimbursement rate under this subdivision must be an amount equal
26 to the district's opportunity index score minus 59, multiplied by
27 the band adjustment factor applicable to this subdivision, plus
28 39.0%.
29 (e) A district or public school academy with an opportunity
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) e6cdo0

124
1 index score greater than or equal to 73 but less than 85 must be
2 assigned to band 5 and shall receive reimbursement under subsection
3 (3) at a rate of at least 42.0% and less than 47.0%. The

5 to the district's opportunity index score minus 73, multiplied by
6 the band adjustment factor applicable to this subdivision, plus
7 42.0%.
8 (f) A district or public school academy with an opportunity
9 index score greater than or equal to 85 must be assigned to band 6
10 and shall receive reimbursement under subsection (3) at a rate of
11 47.0%.
12 (g) As used in this subsection, "band adjustment factor" means
13 an amount equal to the difference between the lowest and highest
14 reimbursement bounds for each band, divided by the number of
15 possible opportunity index scores in that band.

5 (a) "At-risk pupil" means a pupil in grades pre-K to 12 for

24 social studies content area assessment.
25 (x) Is a pupil who is at risk of not meeting the district's or
26 public school academy's core academic curricular objectives in
27 English language arts or mathematics, as demonstrated on local
28 assessments.
29 (b) "Economically disadvantaged" means a pupil who has been
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) e6cdo0

133
1 determined eligible for free or reduced-price meals as determined
2 under the Richard B. Russell national school lunch act, 42 USC 1751
3 to 1769j; who is in a household receiving supplemental nutrition
4 assistance program or temporary assistance for needy families
5 assistance; or who is homeless, migrant, or in foster care, as
6 reported to the center.
7 (c) "English language learner" means limited English
8 proficient pupils who speak a language other than English as their
9 primary language and have difficulty speaking, reading, writing, or
10 understanding English as reported to the center.
11 (d) "Support category building" means a school building
12 identified by the state accountability system as a comprehensive
13 support and improvement school, additional targeted support school,
14 or targeted support and improvement school.

23 (3) The department shall develop and implement a competitive
24 grant program for districts and nonpublic schools to assist in

29 school is based on the number of meals served by the district or
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) e6cdo0

139
1 nonpublic school during the previous school year under the Richard
2 B. Russell national school lunch act, 42 USC 1751 to 1769j. The
3 department shall collaborate with the Michigan department of
4 agriculture and rural development to provide training to newly
5 participating schools and nonpublic schools and electronic
6 information on Michigan agriculture.
7 (4) The goals of the program under this section include
8 improving daily nutrition and eating habits for children through
9 the school and child care settings while investing in Michigan's
10 agricultural and related food business economy.
11 (5) A district or nonpublic school that receives a grant under
12 this section shall use those funds for the costs incurred by the
13 district or the nonpublic school to do both of the following:
14 (a) Purchase whole or minimally processed fruits, vegetables,
15 and legumes that meet both of the following:
16 (i) For each fiscal year, were purchased for use in meals and
17 supportive activities as part of the United States Department of
18 Agriculture child nutrition programs provided between October 1
19 through September 30 of that fiscal year.
20 (ii) Are grown in this state and, if minimally processed, are
21 also processed in this state.
22 (b) Pay for labor and food transportation of locally grown
23 fruits, vegetables, and legumes that directly support the
24 activities and goals of the program described in this section. The
25 amount paid under this subdivision must not exceed 25% of the grant
26 award.
27 (6) For Michigan-grown fruits, vegetables, and legumes that
28 satisfy the requirements of subsection (5), the department shall
29 make matching reimbursements in an amount not to exceed 10 cents
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) e6cdo0

140
1 for every school meal that is served as part of the United States
2 Department of Agriculture's child nutrition programs.
3 (7) In awarding grants under this section, the department
4 shall work in consultation with Michigan-based farm-to-school
5 resource organizations to develop scoring criteria that assess an
6 applicant's ability to procure Michigan-grown products, prepare and
7 menu Michigan-grown products, promote and market Michigan-grown
8 products, and submit letters of intent from districts or nonpublic
9 schools on plans for educational activities that promote the goals
10 of the program.
11 (8) The department shall give preference to districts or
12 nonpublic schools that propose educational activities that meet 1
13 or more of the following: promote healthy food activities; have
14 clear educational objectives; involve parents or the community;
15 connect to a school's or child care center's farm-to-school or
16 farm-to-early-child-care procurement activities; and market and
17 promote the program, leading to increased pupil knowledge and
18 consumption of Michigan-grown products. The department shall give
19 stronger weighting and consideration to applications with robust
20 marketing and promotional activities.

29 schools, between districts, or with agricultural businesses and

23 complex student mental health needs.
24 (e) Purchasing and implementing an online behavioral health

8 (j) Coordination with local law enforcement.

23 for the Deaf and Blind must agree to be subject to a comprehensive

27 upon request in the event of a mass casualty event, and must agree

CRM H06167'26 (H-1) e6cdo0

158
1 any privilege or rights held by any individual, including employees
2 of the recipient. All of the following apply to a comprehensive
3 investigation described in this subsection:
4 (a) The comprehensive investigation will assess the
5 circumstances surrounding the mass casualty event, including, but
6 not limited to:
7 (i) Emergency response effectiveness.
8 (ii) Compliance with safety protocols.
9 (iii) Communication procedures.
10 (iv) Any factors contributing to the incident.
11 (b) The governor shall designate an appropriate person or
12 investigative law enforcement entity to conduct the comprehensive
13 investigation. This person or investigative entity may include, but
14 is not limited to, state law enforcement agencies, independent
15 review boards, or specially appointed task forces. The person or
16 designated investigative law enforcement entity designated by the
17 governor has the authority to do all of the following:
18 (i) Access relevant records and data from the district.
19 (ii) Interview witnesses and district personnel involved.
20 (iii) Issue findings and recommendations based on the
21 investigation.
22 (c) The person or investigative law enforcement entity
23 designated in subdivision (b) shall prepare a detailed report of
24 its findings and submit the report to the governor and relevant
25 legislative committees within 90 days following the conclusion of
26 the investigation. The report must include recommendations for
27 preventing future incidents and improving school safety protocols.

21 takes place.

23 injured individuals necessitating emergency services.an incident of
24 targeted violence that occurs on school grounds or at a school-
25 sponsored event, that is intentional, and that results in a
26 fatality or significant bodily injuries requiring medical attention
27 to 3 or more individuals.

6 by the district.

8 sanctioned by the district.

16 alternative to great start readiness program policies and
17 regulations if the great start readiness program demonstrates to
18 the satisfaction of the department of lifelong education,
19 advancement, and potential that the great start readiness program
20 is meeting the requirements of the Head Start national performance
21 standards.

25 (17) Each grant recipient shall enroll children identified
26 under subsection (8)(b) according to how far the child's household
27 income is below 400% of the federal poverty guidelines by ranking
28 each applicant child's household income from lowest to highest and
29 dividing the applicant children into quintiles based on how far the
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) e6cdo0

172
1 child's household income is below 400% of the federal poverty
2 guidelines, and then enrolling children in the quintile with the
3 lowest household income before enrolling children in the quintile
4 with the next lowest household income until slots are completely
5 filled. If the grant recipient determines that all eligible
6 children are being served and that there are no children on the
7 waiting list who live with families with a household income that is
8 equal to or less than 400% of the federal poverty guidelines, the
9 grant recipient may then enroll children who live with families
10 with a household income that is greater than 400% of the federal
11 poverty guidelines. The enrollment process must consider income and
12 risk factors, such that children determined with higher need are
13 enrolled before children with lesser need. For purposes of this
14 subsection, all age-eligible children served in foster care or who
15 are experiencing homelessness or who have individualized education
16 programs recommending placement in an inclusive preschool setting
17 are considered to live with families with household income equal to
18 or less than 400% of the federal poverty guidelines regardless of
19 actual family income and are prioritized for enrollment within the
20 lowest quintile. This subsection does not apply after September 30,
21 2025.

25 (19) (23) As used in this section:
26 (a) "Child care center" means that term as defined in section
27 1 of 1973 PA 116, MCL 722.111.
28 (b) "Eligible great start readiness program options" means a
29 program option that operates on a school-day, part-day, or extended
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) e6cdo0

178
1 schedule length, as determined by the department of lifelong
2 education, advancement, and potential. The department of lifelong
3 education, advancement, and potential must maintain and publish on
4 its website requirements for each eligible schedule length,
5 including the minimum day length, the minimum number of days per
6 week, and the minimum number of weeks per year. These programs may
7 be blended with Head Start programs, if allowable by federal rules
8 and regulations.
9 (c) "Federal poverty guidelines" means the guidelines
10 published annually in the Federal Register by the United States
11 Department of Health and Human Services under its authority to
12 revise the poverty line under 42 USC 9902.
13 (d) "GSRP extended program" means a program that operates for
14 at least the same length of day as a district's first grade program
15 for a minimum of 5 days per week, 36 weeks per year.
16 (e) "GSRP/Head Start extended blended program" means a program
17 funded under this section and a Head Start program that are
18 combined for an extended program.
19 (f) "GSRP/Head Start school-day blended program" means a part-
20 day program funded under this section and a Head Start program,
21 which are combined for a school-day program.
22 (g) "Licensed child care center" means a child care center
23 that has been issued a license under 1973 PA 116, MCL 722.111 to
24 722.128, to operate a child care center.
25 (h) "Part-day program" means a program that operates at least
26 4 days per week, 30 weeks per year, for at least 3 hours of
27 teacher-child contact time per day but for fewer hours of teacher-
28 child contact time per day than a school-day program.
29 (i) "School-day program" means a program that operates for at
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) e6cdo0

179
1 least the same length of day as a district's first grade program
2 for a minimum of 4 days per week, 30 weeks per year. A classroom
3 that offers a school-day program must enroll all children for the
4 school day to be considered a school-day program.

7 percentage of pupils in the district who are eligible for free and
8 reduced-priced meals as determined under the Richard B. Russell
9 national school lunch act, 42 USC 1751 to 1769j, where eligible
10 entities will provide before-and-after-school or summer school
11 programs.
12 (c) Whether the application provides services for the full
13 school year.
14 (d) The applicant's track record for providing quality,
15 affordable before-and-after-school or summer school services.
16 (e) Whether an applicant serving children in any of grades K
17 to 8 is a licensed child care organization, is an entity that has
18 an active application to be a licensed child care organization, or
19 has implemented the Michigan Out-of-School Time Standards of
20 Quality if the applicant is an exempt entity and , beginning in
21 2025-2026, serves at least 15 school-age youth at a single location

29 (10) (13) As used in this section:
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) e6cdo0

215
1 (a) "Dyslexia" means both of the following:
2 (i) A specific learning disorder that is neurobiological in
3 origin and characterized by difficulties with accurate or fluent
4 word recognition and by poor spelling and decoding abilities that
5 typically result from a deficit in the phonological component of
6 language that is often unexpected in relation to other cognitive
7 abilities and the provision of effective classroom instruction.
8 (ii) A specific learning disorder that may include secondary
9 consequences, such as problems in reading comprehension and a
10 reduced reading experience that can impede the growth of vocabulary
11 and background knowledge and lead to social, emotional, and
12 behavioral difficulties.
13 (b) "Evidence-based" means an activity, program, process,
14 service, strategy, or intervention that demonstrates statistically
15 significant effects on improving pupil outcomes or other relevant
16 outcomes and that meets at least both of the following:
17 (i) At least 1 of the following:
18 (A) Is based on strong evidence from at least 1 well-designed
19 and well-implemented experimental study.
20 (B) Is based on moderate evidence from at least 1 well-
21 designed and well-implemented quasi-experimental study.
22 (C) Is based on promising evidence from at least 1 well-
23 designed and well-implemented correlational study with statistical
24 controls for selection bias.
25 (D) Demonstrates a rationale based on high-quality research
26 findings or positive evaluation that the activity, program,
27 process, service, strategy, or intervention is likely to improve
28 pupil outcomes or other relevant outcomes.
29 (ii) Includes ongoing efforts to examine the effects of the
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) e6cdo0

216
1 activity, program, process, service, strategy, or intervention.
2 (c) "Explicit" means direct and deliberate instruction through
3 continuous pupil-teacher interaction that includes teacher
4 modeling, guided practice, and independent practice.
5 (d) "Fluency" means the ability to read with speed, accuracy,
6 and proper expression.
7 (e) "Multi-tiered system of supports" means a comprehensive
8 framework that includes 3 distinct tiers of instructional support
9 and is composed of a collection of evidence-based strategies
10 designed to meet the individual needs and assets of a whole pupil
11 at all achievement levels.
12 (f) "Phonemic awareness" means the conscious awareness of all
13 of the following:
14 (i) Individual speech sounds, including, but not limited to,
15 consonants and vowels, in spoken syllables.
16 (ii) The ability to consciously manipulate through, including,
17 but not limited to, matching, blending, segmenting, deleting, or
18 substituting, individual speech sounds described in subparagraph
19 (i).
20 (iii) All levels of the speech sound system, including, but not
21 limited to, word boundaries, rhyme recognition, stress patterns,
22 syllables, onset-rime units, and phonemes.
23 (g) "Phonological" means relating to the system of contrastive
24 relationships among the speech sounds that constitute the
25 fundamental components of a language.
26 (h) "Progress monitoring" means the assessing of students'
27 academic performance, quantifying students' rates of improvement or
28 progress toward goals, and determining how students are responding
29 to instruction.
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) e6cdo0

217
1 (i) "Rapid automatized naming (RAN)" means a task that
2 measures how quickly individuals can name objects; pictures;
3 colors; or symbols, including letters and digits, aloud, which can
4 predict later reading abilities for preliterate children.

10 (2) The department shall continue the committee for literacy
11 achievement that includes representatives of higher education and
12 early childhood literacy educators who have expertise in literacy
13 instruction and research. The committee shall exist until at least
14 September 2029. The department shall appoint the members of the
15 committee for literacy achievement. The department shall develop a
16 process to identify and assemble experts for the committee. The
17 primary focus of the committee is to recommend tools and strategies
18 with the goal of increasing student academic outcomes in the area
19 of literacy. The committee is encouraged to collaborate with the
20 Michigan Education Research Institute or other entities with
21 similar expertise on the subject of literacy. The department shall
22 determine the size and benchmarks of the committee. The committee
23 shall do all of the following:
24 (a) Critically evaluate early literacy series and materials.
25 The committee shall evaluate with research-based outcomes, using
26 the committee's expertise in the field of literacy. The committee
27 shall assign grade tiers to the series based on the series'
28 likelihood to increase student literacy outcomes. Priority must be
29 given to series that already have a demonstrated history of
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) e6cdo0

218
1 increasing student outcomes. The committee may perform an
2 evaluation under this subdivision based on submissions from a
3 vendor, but must independently verify the validity of information
4 provided by the vendor. The committee is also strongly encouraged
5 to evaluate available materials not submitted to the department by
6 a vendor.
7 (b) Critically evaluate literacy professional development. The
8 committee shall evaluate with research-based outcomes, using the
9 committee's expertise in the field of literacy. The committee shall
10 assign grade tiers to the professional development based on the
11 professional development's likelihood to increase student literacy
12 outcomes. Priority must be given to professional development that
13 already has a demonstrated history of increasing student outcomes.

17 is also strongly encouraged to evaluate available materials not
18 submitted to the department by a vendor.

20 services the committee determines to have a high likelihood or
21 demonstrated history of increasing student literacy outcomes. The
22 committee may perform an evaluation under this subdivision based on
23 submissions from a vendor, but must independently verify the
24 validity of information provided by the vendor. The committee is
25 also strongly encouraged to evaluate available materials not

10 (b) The reasonability that the applicant will be able to meet
11 the stated outcome benchmarks.
12 (c) Other criteria, as determined by the department, to ensure
13 grants are awarded to the most impactful applicants.
14 (4) If the amount allocated in subsection (1) is not
15 sufficient to fully fund all applicants under subsection (2), the
16 department shall prioritize grant awards to eligible tutoring
17 partners who will work with districts with the lowest levels of
18 literacy proficiency, as determined by student scores on the
19 Michigan student test of educational progress (M-STEP).
20 (5) The department may approve applications from eligible
21 tutoring partners that offer innovative ways to reach students at a
22 lower cost by waiving elements of the high-impact tutoring
23 definition. This must include, but is not limited to, offering
24 virtual programs. In such cases, the applicant must provide
25 evidence of program impact and implement an outcomes-based contract
26 model.
27 (6) It is the intent of the legislature that awarded
28 applicants will receive funding over a 2-year period, with up to
29 50% of the awarded amount being made available in 2026-2027 and the
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) e6cdo0

228
1 remaining amount being made available in 2027-2028.
2 (7) It is the intent of the legislature that the work
3 associated with the grant application, oversight, and
4 administration of awards under this section be performed primarily
5 by the eligible tutoring partner and not by districts that have
6 agreed to receive high-impact tutoring.
7 (8) If a district or intermediate district agrees to receive
8 high-impact tutoring from an eligible tutoring partner, the
9 district must do all of the following:
10 (a) Work with the eligible tutoring partner to identify
11 students that would benefit from the program. The district or
12 intermediate district must communicate with parents or legal
13 guardians of identified students to describe the available program,
14 outline possible student outcomes, and encourage student
15 participation.
16 (b) To the extent possible, integrate the high-impact tutoring
17 program with the district's continuous improvement plan.
18 (c) If the district or intermediate district is the fiscal
19 agent for the eligible tutoring partner, except as otherwise
20 provided in this subdivision, forward the amount awarded to the
21 tutoring partner. The designated fiscal agent may retain up to 0.5%
22 of the funding awarded to the tutoring partner for administrative
23 purposes.
24 (9) Both of the following apply to the funds allocated under
25 this section:
26 (a) The department shall make award determinations and
27 notifications by not later than November 15, 2026.
28 (b) The department shall make initial payments to identified
29 fiscal agents by not later than January 1, 2027. Identified fiscal
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) e6cdo0

229
1 agents must forward funding to eligible tutoring partners by not
2 later than January 15, 2027.
3 (10) Eligible tutoring partners shall begin providing high-
4 impact tutoring in recipient districts and intermediate districts
5 before the end of the 2026-2027 school year.
6 (11) The funds allocated under this section for 2026-2027 are
7 a work project appropriation, and any unexpended funds for 2026-
8 2027 are carried forward into 2027-2028. The purpose of the work
9 project is to provide high-impact tutoring through eligible
10 tutoring partners. The estimated completion date of the work
11 project is September 30, 2029.
12 (12) From the allocation in subsection (1), there is allocated
13 $600,000.00 to Clinton County RESA to work with the Michigan
14 Association of Intermediate School Administrators to do all of the
15 following:
16 (a) Provide a report on high-impact tutoring programs
17 implemented with the funding allocated under subsection (1).
18 (b) Develop and provide technical assistance to districts in
19 selecting high-impact tutoring strategies and integrating these
20 strategies into district Michigan Integrated Continuous Improvement
21 Process (MICIP) plans. Developing and providing technical
22 assistance may include the design, review, and integration of
23 eligible tutoring programs within the MiStrategyBank.
24 (c) Collect, aggregate, and report data in collaboration with
25 the MAISA Michigan Collaborative Hub. An amount not to exceed
26 $300,000.00 of the funds allocated under this subsection may be
27 used for this purpose.
28 (d) Provide an annual report of the impact of high-impact
29 tutoring programs to the office of the governor, the senate and
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) e6cdo0

230
1 house education committees, the senate and house appropriations
2 subcommittees on school aid, and the state budget director.
3 (13) As used in this section:
4 (a) "Eligible tutoring partner" means a tutoring provider,
5 including, but not limited to, those created by for-profit vendors,
6 nonprofit vendors, intermediate districts, districts, and the
7 Michigan Schools for the Deaf and Blind, that provides high-impact
8 tutoring and meets at least 1 of the following:
9 (i) Is included on the ProvenTutoring list maintained by the
10 Center for Research and Reform in Education at Johns Hopkins
11 University.
12 (ii) Has a Tutoring Program Design Badge as awarded by the
13 National Student Support Accelerator, a program of the SCALE
14 Initiative at Stanford University.
15 (iii) Is a vetted high-impact tutoring program included in the
16 MiStrategyBank. If necessary, the Michigan Association of
17 Intermediate School Administrators may convene a committee to
18 review tutoring programs for inclusion in the MiStrategyBank.
19 (b) "High-impact tutoring" means, except as otherwise provided
20 in this section, in-person tutoring that meets all of the
21 following:
22 (i) Occurs during or after the school day.
23 (ii) Is individualized with tutor-student ratios not higher
24 than 1:4.
25 (iii) Provides intensive support with a minimum of 2 sessions
26 per week.
27 (iv) Uses a structured, evidence-based curriculum designed for
28 tutoring with formative assessments to track progress.
29 (v) Uses trained tutors.
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) e6cdo0

231
1 (vi) Includes ongoing assessment and feedback.
2 Sec. 35q. (1) From the state school aid fund money
3 appropriated in section 11, there is allocated for 2026-2027 an
4 amount not to exceed $40,000,000.00 for a pilot program that
5 reimburses parents and legal guardians for the costs of private
6 tutoring.
7 (2) The department shall partner with not more than 20
8 districts through a grant process to implement the pilot program
9 described in this section. The department shall inform all
10 participating districts of their award under the pilot program by
11 not later than December 1, 2026. All of the following apply to the
12 pilot program:
13 (a) To participate in the pilot program, the district must be
14 in an opportunity index band, as determined under section 31a, of 4
15 or higher.
16 (b) The pilot program must include both urban and rural
17 districts and include districts from all geographic regions of this
18 state.
19 (3) After receiving funding under this section, districts
20 shall allow parents and legal guardians to submit an application
21 for funds to reimburse the parent or legal guardian for the costs
22 of private tutoring. Districts shall provide notice to parents and
23 legal guardians about the pilot program, the application process,
24 and a list of potential private tutors not later than 30 days prior
25 to the application being made available. An application by a parent
26 or legal guardian under this subsection must include at least all
27 of the following:
28 (a) The cost of the private tutoring program.
29 (b) The number of hours per week that the parent or legal
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) e6cdo0

232
1 guardian's child will receive tutoring in the program.
2 (4) Districts shall prioritize funding for students that are
3 economically disadvantaged and most academically at risk. Districts
4 shall cover 100% of the direct costs for private tutoring for any
5 child approved to participate in the pilot program for not less
6 than 1 year. Upon approval of an application under subsection (3),
7 a district shall inform the parent or legal guardian of the maximum
8 amount of funding that the district will reimburse for private
9 tutoring.
10 (5) A district may make payments through 1 or more of the
11 following methods:
12 (a) A district may make advance payments to parents and legal
13 guardians to cover tutoring costs. A parent or legal guardian shall
14 submit to the district all receipts necessary to ensure
15 reimbursements being made do not exceed program costs. A district
16 or the department may contact a tutoring provider directly to
17 verify program costs and participation.
18 (b) A district may require a parent or legal guardian to
19 submit a receipt documenting payment before providing
20 reimbursement. A district shall reimburse a parent or legal
21 guardian not later than 2 weeks after receiving proof of payment.
22 (c) A district may reimburse a private tutoring provider
23 directly. The private tutoring provider shall provide to the
24 district records of student attendance in the program.
25 (6) From the funds allocated in subsection (1), there is
26 allocated not more than $200,000.00 to an intermediate district to
27 partner with the department to conduct a study on the outcomes of
28 the pilot program under this section. The study must include, but
29 is not limited to, an analysis of test scores before and after
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) e6cdo0

233
1 participation in the pilot program and a comparison to students
2 that did not participate in the program. The intermediate district
3 shall submit a report on the study to the house and senate
4 subcommittees on school aid, the house and senate fiscal agencies,
5 and the state budget office by not later than September 30, 2028.
6 (7) A district receiving funding under this section may retain
7 2.5% of funds for administration.
8 (8) Notwithstanding section 17b, the department shall make
9 payments under this section on a schedule determined by the
10 department.
11 (9) The funds allocated under this section for 2026-2027 are a
12 work project appropriation, and any unexpended funds for 2026—2027
13 are carried forward into 2027-2028. The purpose of the work project
14 is to continue to implement the pilot program on private tutoring
15 and associated study. The estimated completion date of the work
16 project is September 30, 2028.
17 Sec. 36. (1) From the state school aid fund money appropriated
18 in section 11, there is allocated for 2026-2027 an amount not to
19 exceed $100,000,000.00 for the purposes described in this section,
20 including payments to improve educational outcomes in mathematics.
21 (2) The department shall create the committee for mathematics
22 achievement, which shall include representatives of higher
23 education and mathematics educators who have expertise in
24 mathematics instruction and research. The committee shall exist
25 until at least September 2029. The department shall appoint the
26 members of the committee for mathematics achievement. The
27 department shall develop a process to identify and assemble experts
28 for the committee. The primary focus of the committee is to
29 recommend tools and strategies with the goal of increasing student
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) e6cdo0

234
1 academic outcomes in the area of mathematics. The committee is
2 encouraged to collaborate with entities with expertise on the
3 subject of mathematics. The department shall determine the size and
4 benchmarks of the committee. The committee shall do all of the
5 following:
6 (a) Critically evaluate mathematics curricula and materials.
7 The committee shall evaluate with research-based outcomes, using
8 the committee's expertise in the field of mathematics. The
9 committee shall assign grade tiers to a curriculum based on the
10 curriculum's likelihood of increasing student mathematics outcomes.
11 Priority must be given to curricula that already have a
12 demonstrated history of increasing student outcomes. The committee
13 may perform an evaluation under this subdivision based on
14 submissions from a vendor, but must independently verify the
15 validity of information provided by the vendor. The committee is
16 also strongly encouraged to evaluate available materials not
17 submitted to the department by a vendor.
18 (b) Critically evaluate mathematics professional development.
19 The committee shall evaluate with research-based outcomes, using
20 the committee's expertise in the field of mathematics. The
21 committee shall assign grade tiers to professional development
22 programs based on the professional development program's likelihood
23 of increasing student mathematics outcomes. Priority must be given
24 to professional development programs that already have a
25 demonstrated history of increasing student outcomes. The committee
26 may perform an evaluation under this subdivision based on
27 submissions from a vendor, but must independently verify the
28 validity of information provided by the vendor. The committee is
29 also strongly encouraged to evaluate available materials not
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) e6cdo0

235
1 submitted to the department by a vendor.
2 (c) Critically evaluate other applicable mathematics tools or
3 services the committee determines to have a high likelihood or
4 demonstrated history of increasing student mathematics outcomes.
5 The committee may perform an evaluation under this subdivision
6 based on submissions from a vendor, but must independently verify
7 the validity of information provided by the vendor. The committee
8 is also strongly encouraged to evaluate available materials not
9 submitted to the department by a vendor.
10 (d) Create, maintain, and annually post on a publicly
11 available website a rankings list of all mathematics curricula and
12 other items the committee has evaluated. This includes items from
13 each part of the rankings list, items evaluated in the 2026-2027
14 school year, and any subsequent items evaluated going forward. The
15 committee shall denote on the list whether major changes have been
16 made to a mathematics curriculum or other item on the list since
17 the mathematics curriculum or other item was evaluated by the
18 committee and if the mathematics curriculum or other item has not
19 been evaluated since the last ranking.
20 (e) The committee must select not more than 5 mathematics
21 curricula to recommend for purchase by districts.
22 (3) An individual on the committee for mathematics achievement
23 shall recuse themselves from evaluating mathematics curricula or
24 other items that the individual has helped create, that the
25 individual would benefit from financially, or for which the
26 individual has any other conflict of interest.
27 (4) From the funds allocated in subsection (1), and subject to
28 subsection (6), the department shall make payments to districts and
29 intermediate districts in an amount determined by the department.
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) e6cdo0

8 option who meet the age-eligible criteria of section 32d.

21 section must be accessible throughout this state. Funds received by
22 an association under this section may be used to support the
23 development of assessment tools to measure the needs of students
24 with special education needs in remote learning environments and
25 the effectiveness of various educational methods and tools, in
26 collaboration with the department. Funds under this section may
27 also be utilized to identify any available federal funds for
28 research related to special education in remote learning.

1 a separate account within the state school aid fund for the purpose

12 fund.

27 program.

29 that term as defined in section 3 of the career and technical
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) e6cdo0

1 preparation act, 2000 PA 258, MCL 388.1903.
2 Sec. 61w. (1) From the state school aid fund money
3 appropriated in section 11, there is allocated for 2026-2027 only
4 an amount not to exceed $5,000,000.00 for competitive grants to
5 districts and intermediate districts to support virtual reality
6 simulation programs that enhance and expand existing career and
7 technical education programs.
8 (2) Eligible districts and intermediate districts shall apply
9 for funds under this section on a competitive basis in a form and
10 manner determined by the department.
11 (3) A virtual reality simulation program described in this
12 section must offer tactile, multimodal learning experiences that
13 derive and teach core state standards-aligned concepts and skills
14 for grades 6 to 12 math and science, including middle school math
15 and algebra I. The virtual reality simulation program should
16 immerse students in real-world problems across the economy to build
17 career exposure and relevance, using 3-D models and interactives
18 that visualize and manipulate abstract mathematical concepts that
19 students typically struggle to grasp, retain, and apply.
20 (4) Awards received under this section must be used to support
21 career and technical education programs aligned with high-skill,
22 high-wage, and high-demand occupations across career clusters
23 aligned with local and market needs. A recipient may use an award
24 under this section to support virtual reality simulation programs
25 that reach multiple career clusters.
26 (5) Notwithstanding section 17b, the department shall make
27 payments under this section on a schedule determined by the
28 department.

11 Sec. 67. (1) From the general fund money appropriated in
12 section 11, there is allocated an amount not to exceed
13 $3,000,000.00 for 2025-2026 2026-2027 for college access programs.
14 The programs funded under this section are intended to inform
15 students of college and career options, to provide resources
16 intended to increase the number of pupils who are adequately
17 prepared with the information needed to make informed decisions on
18 college and career, support adult learners, support college
19 completion, and support workforce and employer engagement. The
20 funds appropriated under this section are intended to be used to
21 increase the number of Michigan residents with high-quality degrees
22 or credentials. Funds appropriated under this section must not be
23 used to supplant funding for counselors already funded by
24 districts.
25 (2) The department of lifelong education, advancement, and
26 potential shall administer funds allocated under this section in
27 collaboration with the Michigan college access network. These funds
28 may be used for any of the following purposes:
29 (a) Michigan college access network operations, programming,
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) e6cdo0

289
1 and services to local college access networks.
2 (b) Local college access networks, which are community-based
3 college access/success partnerships committed to increasing the
4 college participation and completion rates within geographically
5 defined communities through a coordinated strategy.
6 (c) The Michigan college advising program, a program intended
7 to place trained, recently graduated college advisors in high
8 schools that serve significant numbers of low-income and first-
9 generation college-going pupils. State funds used for this purpose
10 may not exceed 33% of the total funds available under this
11 subsection.
12 (d) Subgrants of up to $5,000.00 to districts with
13 comprehensive high schools that establish a college access team and
14 implement specific strategies to create a college-going culture in
15 a high school in a form and manner approved by the Michigan college
16 access network and the department of lifelong education,
17 advancement, and potential.
18 (e) The Michigan college access portal, an online one-stop
19 portal to help pupils and families plan and apply for college.
20 (f) Public awareness and outreach campaigns to encourage low-
21 income and first-generation college-going pupils to take necessary
22 steps toward college and to assist pupils and families in
23 completing a timely and accurate free application for federal
24 student aid.
25 (g) Subgrants to postsecondary institutions to recruit, hire,
26 and train college student mentors and college advisors to assist
27 high school pupils in navigating the postsecondary planning and
28 enrollment process.

1 Sec. 94a. (1) There is created within the state budget office
2 in the department of technology, management, and budget the center
3 for educational performance and information. The center shall do
4 all of the following:
5 (a) Coordinate the collection of all data required by state
6 and federal law from districts, intermediate districts, and
7 postsecondary institutions.
8 (b) Create, maintain, and enhance this state's P-20
9 longitudinal data system and ensure that it meets the requirements
10 of subsection (4).
11 (c) Collect data in the most efficient manner possible to
12 reduce the administrative burden on reporting entities, including,
13 but not limited to, electronic transcript services.
14 (d) Create, maintain, and enhance this state's web-based
15 educational portal to provide information to school leaders,
16 teachers, researchers, and the public in compliance with all
17 federal and state privacy laws. Data must include, but are not
18 limited to, all of the following:
19 (i) Data sets that link teachers to student information,
20 allowing districts to assess individual teacher impact on student
21 performance and consider student growth factors in teacher and
22 principal evaluation systems.
23 (ii) Data access or, if practical, data sets, provided for
24 regional data hubs that, in combination with local data, can
25 improve teaching and learning in the classroom.
26 (iii) Research-ready data sets for researchers to perform
27 research that advances this state's educational performance.
28 (e) Provide data in a useful manner to allow state and local
29 policymakers to make informed policy decisions.
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) e6cdo0

297
1 (f) Provide public reports to the residents of this state to
2 allow them to assess allocation of resources and the return on

6 information from districts, intermediate districts, or
7 postsecondary institutions as required under state or federal law
8 shall make arrangements with the center to ensure that the state

29 records so that an individual student may be matched with those
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) e6cdo0

298
1 teachers providing instruction to that student.

4 prepared and recommended those teachers for state certification.

6 improvement and decision-making, including timely reporting to
7 parents, teachers, and school leaders on student achievement.
8 (f) Ensures the reasonable quality, validity, and reliability

8 center shall cooperate with the department to ensure that this
9 state is in compliance with federal law and is maximizing
10 opportunities for increased federal funding to improve education in
11 this state.

24 technology-based instructional tools and resources.

1 providers should be monitored and evaluated to ensure a quality

16 (12) As used in this section:
17 (a) "Blended learning" means a hybrid instructional delivery
18 model where pupils are provided content, instruction, and
19 assessment, in part at a supervised educational facility away from
20 home where the pupil and a teacher with a valid Michigan teaching
21 certificate are in the same physical location and in part through
22 internet-connected learning environments with some degree of pupil
23 control over time, location, and pace of instruction.
24 (b) "Cyber school" means a full-time instructional program of
25 virtual courses for pupils that may or may not require attendance
26 at a physical school location.
27 (c) "Virtual course" means a course of study that is capable
28 of generating a credit or a grade and that is provided in an
29 interactive learning environment in which the majority of the
CRM H06167'26 (H-1) e6cdo0

313
1 curriculum is delivered using the internet and in which pupils are
2 separated from their instructor or teacher of record by time or
3 location, or both.
